name: Career Awards at the Scientific Interface (CASI)
awardAmount: $560,000 over 5 years (bridges advanced postdoc + first 3 years of faculty)
duration: 5 years (up to 2 years postdoc + 3 years faculty)
deadlineInfo: Annual. For the 2027 cycle: LOI Aug 14, 2026 (3pm EDT); full application (by invitation) Jan 11, 2027; interviews Apr 2027; award start Jul 1, 2027.
eligibility: PhD in mathematics, physics, chemistry, computer science, statistics, or engineering (biology PhDs allowed with strong demonstrated quantitative expertise). 12-60 months of postdoctoral experience; cannot hold or have accepted a tenure-track faculty position; may not simultaneously hold an NIH K99/R00. At least one first-author publication. US/Canadian citizens, permanent residents, or temporary residents with valid visa; must be at a US/Canada nonprofit 501(c)(3). No institutional nomination required (open application).
description: Flagship BWF award explicitly designed for quantitative/computational scientists (math, physics, CS, statistics, engineering) transitioning into biology and biomedical research, funding the postdoc-to-faculty transition.
relevanceToUser: The single best fit: it is purpose-built for computer-science/statistics/ML-trained researchers moving into biomedical problems. A computational oncology / blood-cancer ML program would be squarely on-theme, and the large 5-year package uniquely bridges the postdoc-to-first-faculty gap.

name: Postdoctoral Enrichment Program (PDEP)
awardAmount: $60,000 over 3 years ($20,000/year for research supplies and career-development/training)
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2026 cycle full proposal due Jan 20, 2026; award start Sep 1, 2026.
eligibility: Career-development supplement for underrepresented postdoctoral fellows (program targets groups historically underrepresented in biomedical science). No more than 48 months of postdoctoral experience and no more than 5 years from PhD at the deadline; must have a funded postdoc position secured at a US/Canada degree-granting institution; US or Canadian citizens. Conducting biological/biomedical research. Up to 5 extra Revson-funded awards for NYC-metro institutions.
description: A career-enrichment grant (not a full salary award) that supplements underrepresented postdocs' training, travel, coursework, and supplies to strengthen the path to a faculty position.
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the researcher is at the postdoc stage and from an underrepresented background; funds enrichment for biomedical/computational-biology postdocs. Modest dollar amount but a prestigious line for early-career development toward a computational-oncology faculty career.

name: Pew Scholars Program in the Biomedical Sciences (Pew Biomedical Scholars)
awardAmount: $300,000 total ($75,000/year for 4 years); PI salary use capped at ~$12,500/yr ($50,000 total)
deadlineInfo: Annual, nomination-based. 2026 cycle: institutional nominations due ~May 13, 2026; applications close ~Sep 3, 2026.
eligibility: Doctorate (PhD, MD, or equivalent) in biomedical sciences, medicine, or a related field including engineering or the physical sciences. As of the application cutoff (Sep 3, 2025 for the 2026 class) must hold a full-time, tenure-track assistant professor appointment and run an independent lab; research/adjunct/visiting/instructor titles are ineligible. One nominee per invited institution (institutional nomination required); may apply a maximum of two times. Does not fund clinical trials.
description: Prestigious unrestricted early-career award for newly independent assistant professors doing innovative biomedical research; flexible funds support high-risk, creative science.
relevanceToUser: Well suited for a newly-appointed assistant professor; the physical-sciences/engineering pathway means computational and ML-driven biomedical research (including computational oncology) is eligible. Unrestricted funds are ideal for methods-focused labs. Requires institutional nomination (your institution would nominate one candidate).

name: Searle Scholars Program
awardAmount: $450,000 over 3 years ($150,000/year); ~15 awards per year
deadlineInfo: Annual, nomination-based. 2026 competition submission deadline ~Sep 30, 2026; awards activated Jul 1. First tenure-track start must be on/after May 1 of ~2 years prior (e.g., May 1, 2024 for the 2026 cycle; May 1, 2025 for 2027).
eligibility: Pursuing an independent research career in biochemistry, cell biology, genetics, immunology, neuroscience, pharmacology, and related areas of chemistry, medicine, and the biological sciences. Must have begun a first tenure-track (or nearest-equivalent) assistant-professor appointment within the defined recent window. Restricted to nominees from participating institutions (institutional nomination required). Does not ordinarily support purely clinical research but allows programs mixing basic and clinical components.
description: Highly selective award for scientists in the first year or two of an independent faculty appointment, funding bold biomedical research with flexible support.
relevanceToUser: Strong fit for a first-time assistant professor doing mechanism-oriented cancer/blood-cancer biology with computational methods; the eligible fields include genetics, cell biology, and immunology, which encompass computational oncology and leukemia/lymphoma research. Requires institutional nomination and a very recent first faculty appointment.

name: Rita Allen Foundation Scholars
awardAmount: Up to $110,000/year for up to 5 years (up to ~$550,000 total)
deadlineInfo: Annual, nomination-based. Recent cycle: nominees' letter of inquiry due ~early Sep, full application ~mid-Oct (e.g., LOI Sep 3, 2025; full app Oct 15, 2025). One nominee per invited institution. (Separate Award in Pain has its own open call, Oct-Dec.)
eligibility: Early-career investigators in the biomedical sciences with focus areas of cancer, immunology, and neuroscience (plus a separate pain track). Must be legally employed at time of application at a US degree-granting or research institution that is an invited participant in the Scholars Program; one nominated candidate per eligible institution per year. Intended for investigators early in their independent careers who have not yet received major private awards.
description: Award for exceptional early-career biomedical investigators, with cancer as one of the three core disease focus areas, supporting pioneering research over up to 5 years.
relevanceToUser: Directly relevant because cancer (and cancer immunology) is a named priority area; an AI/ML computational-oncology and blood-cancer program fits well. Requires institutional nomination and targets investigators who have not yet won large private awards, making it a good early-independence target.

name: Beckman Young Investigator (BYI) Program
awardAmount: $600,000 over 4 years ($150,000/year); no indirect costs; year 3-4 contingent on demonstrated progress
deadlineInfo: Annual. For the 2027 program: LOI due Jul 31, 2026; full application (by invitation) Jan 11, 2027; interviews late March.
eligibility: Within the first 4 years of a tenure-track (or equivalent independent) research appointment at a US academic/nonprofit institution in the chemical and life sciences (tenure-track start on/after ~Jan 1 four years before the LOI). Must be a US citizen or permanent resident before the interview. No more than $225,000 in annualized direct external funding at application or in award year one. May apply at most twice (counting LOIs).
description: Supports the most promising early-career faculty pursuing innovative, high-risk research in the chemical and life sciences, favoring new methods and tools.
relevanceToUser: Fits an early-career faculty member (first 4 years) developing novel computational/ML methods and tools within the life sciences, including cancer applications. Note the US citizen/permanent-resident requirement and the cap on existing external funding, which favors newly independent PIs.

name: Clinical Scientist Development Award (CSDA)
funder: Doris Duke Foundation (Doris Duke Charitable Foundation)
awardAmount: ~$495,000 over 3 years ($150,000/year direct + 10% indirect); see site for current cycle
deadlineInfo: Annual when open, but cycle timing varies — see site to confirm the current cycle. Historically: pre-proposal ~November, invited full proposal ~March, award start Jul 1.
eligibility: Early-career physician-scientists holding an MD, DO, MD/PhD, or foreign equivalent, at the assistant professor level at a US degree-granting institution, transitioning to independent clinical research. Per Doris Duke's will, no experiments using animals or animal-derived tissues/cell lines are supported. Focused on clinical (human-subjects) research.
description: Mentored-to-independent transition award providing protected research time and funding for junior physician-scientist faculty pursuing patient-oriented clinical research.
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the researcher is a physician-scientist (MD/DO/MD-PhD) doing clinical/patient-oriented oncology or hematologic-malignancy research; a pure-computational PhD without a clinical degree would not be eligible. The animal/cell-line restriction means only human-data (clinical, genomic) computational work qualifies.

name: Klingenstein-Simons Fellowship Awards in the Neurosciences
funder: Esther A. & Joseph Klingenstein Fund and Simons Foundation
deadlineInfo: Annual. Application portal opens ~Sep 15 and closes ~Feb 1; awards announced early June; start Jul 1.
eligibility: Early-career investigators (typically in the first years of an independent/assistant-professor appointment) conducting basic or clinical neuroscience research. Priority to promising scientists who have not yet received substantial private funding. Open application (no institutional nomination requirement).
description: Award for outstanding early-career neuroscientists pursuing innovative basic or clinical neuroscience research.
relevanceToUser: Included for completeness of the cluster, but almost certainly NOT a fit: it funds neuroscience exclusively, so AI/ML computational oncology and blood-cancer research would be out of scope unless the project were reframed around neural systems.

name: Smart Health and Biomedical Research in the Era of Artificial Intelligence and Advanced Data Science (SCH)
awardAmount: Up to $1,200,000 total (up to $300,000/year for up to 4 years); ~$15-20M/year program, 10-16 awards/year
deadlineInfo: Annual. Most recent full-proposal deadline Oct 3, 2025 (NSF 25-542); expect a similar fall cycle in 2026 — verify current solicitation on the NSF page.
eligibility: No PI restrictions; open to US colleges/universities and non-profit non-academic research organizations. An investigator may appear on no more than two proposals per deadline. Projects must make fundamental contributions to two or more disciplines (e.g., CS/AI + biomedical). Traditional disease-centric clinical trials are out of scope.
description: High-risk, high-reward interdisciplinary program (NSF+NIH) funding transformative AI, data-science, and engineering methods that address biomedical and public-health challenges.
relevanceToUser: Best-fit NSF mechanism for AI/ML applied to computational oncology. Rewards projects that pair novel ML/statistical methods with a biomedical problem such as leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma or bone-marrow-failure data, exactly the two-discipline framing this program requires.

name: CISE: Future Computing Research (Future CoRe) — includes IIS core programs (Robust Intelligence; Information Integration and Informatics)
funder: National Science Foundation (Directorate for Computer and Information Science and Engineering, CISE)
awardAmount: Up to $1,000,000 total (guidance: typically $150,000-$250,000/year, not to exceed $300,000 in any single year)
deadlineInfo: Proposals accepted anytime with two annual target dates: 2nd Thursday in September (Sept 11, 2025) and 1st Thursday in February (Feb 5, 2026); recurring annually.
eligibility: US institutions of higher education, non-profit research organizations, and federally recognized Tribal Nations. An individual may be PI/co-PI/senior personnel on at most 2 Future CoRe proposals per 12 months.
description: The consolidated CISE core research solicitation (NSF 25-543, replacing the former CISE/IIS Core Programs) funding foundational computing research including AI/ML (Robust Intelligence) and data/information integration (III).
relevanceToUser: The core CISE pathway for a statistics/ML faculty PI to fund methods research (Robust Intelligence for ML foundations; Information Integration and Informatics for data-driven/biomedical data integration). Methods can be motivated by cancer-genomics/clinical data while remaining CS-core.

name: Faculty Early Career Development Program (CAREER)
awardAmount: Minimum $400,000 over 5 years for most directorates; minimum $500,000 for BIO, ENG, and Polar Programs. No maximum.
deadlineInfo: Annual, fourth Wednesday in July (July 22, 2026). Current solicitation NSF 22-586 (covers submissions through 2026).
eligibility: Must, as of the deadline, hold a doctoral degree in an NSF-supported field, hold at least a 50% tenure-track (or equivalent) assistant-professor position, be untenured, and not have previously received a CAREER award. One proposal per PI per competition; no co-PIs.
description: NSF's flagship early-career award for tenure-track faculty integrating an ambitious research program with education/outreach; submitted to a specific NSF program (e.g., a CISE/IIS or Smart Health program).
relevanceToUser: Premier funding for a newly-independent your institution-track ML/statistics faculty member. A CAREER submitted to CISE (Robust Intelligence) or Smart Health can build a 5-year agenda on AI methods for hematologic-malignancy data.

name: NSF Graduate Research Fellowship Program (GRFP)
awardAmount: $37,000 annual stipend to the fellow + $16,000 cost-of-education allowance to the institution ($53,000/year), for 3 years of support
duration: 3 years of support used within a 5-year fellowship period
deadlineInfo: Annual. For the NSF 25-547 cycle, deadlines were by field in November 2025 (e.g., Computer Science Nov 12, Life Sciences Nov 10, Engineering Nov 13); references due Nov 7, 2025. Expect an equivalent Oct/Nov 2026 cycle.
eligibility: Must be a US citizen, US national, or permanent resident at the deadline; enrolled or intending to enroll full-time in a research-based master's or doctoral STEM program; must have completed less than one academic year of graduate study. Clinical/health-professional degree programs are NOT eligible (computational/quantitative fields are).
description: Prestigious portable fellowship supporting early-stage US graduate students pursuing research-based STEM degrees, providing three years of stipend and tuition support.
relevanceToUser: Directly applicable to a first- or early-year PhD student in the lab (US citizen/PR) doing ML/computational biology. Frame the research as quantitative/computational methods (not a clinical degree) to stay eligible; cancer-data applications are fine.

name: Mathematical Sciences Postdoctoral Research Fellowships (MSPRF)
funder: National Science Foundation (Division of Mathematical Sciences, MPS)
awardAmount: $5,833/month full-time stipend (up to $70,000/year); ~$190,000 total providing 24 months of stipend support (18 academic + 6 summer months)
duration: 48-month fellowship period (24 months of paid support)
deadlineInfo: Annual, third Wednesday in October (Oct 21, 2026). Current solicitation NSF 23-603. ~35-45 awards/year.
eligibility: US citizen, national, or permanent resident; may not have held the doctorate more than 2 years as of January 1 of the award year; may not have been a prior PI on NSF awards (graduate fellowships excepted). Research must be in mathematics or statistics, with applications to other disciplines explicitly allowed.
description: Individual postdoctoral fellowship in the mathematical/statistical sciences, portable to a host institution, supporting research and career development.
relevanceToUser: Well-suited for a statistics/ML PhD moving to a postdoc: the program funds statistics with applications to other disciplines, so methodological work motivated by cancer/blood-cancer data qualifies as long as the intellectual core is statistical/mathematical.

name: CISE Graduate Fellowships (CSGrad4US)
careerStages: prospective PhD student (returning to school after work experience)
awardAmount: $37,000 annual stipend + $16,000 cost-of-education allowance (~$159,000 over 3 years), plus mentorship in the application year
deadlineInfo: Annual; current solicitation NSF 24-033 (successor to the archived NSF 23-074). Verify the current-year deadline on the NSF/CRA page.
eligibility: US citizen, national, or permanent resident; bachelor's or master's degree holders with post-degree work experience who have not been enrolled in a degree program recently and intend to start a CS/computer-engineering/information-science research doctorate. Field limited to computing disciplines.
description: Fellowship encouraging people who have been in the workforce to return to pursue a research-based PhD in a computing field, providing GRFP-equivalent funding plus application mentoring.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if a lab candidate is coming from industry/work experience into a computing PhD on AI/ML for health data — an alternative to GRFP for non-traditional applicants. Note the strict computing-field and no-recent-enrollment requirements.

name: National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Research Institutes
funder: National Science Foundation (with multiple federal/industry partners)
awardAmount: Large center-scale awards (historically ~$16-20M over 5 years per institute); see site — amount not listed on the current program page
deadlineInfo: No open competition as of mid-2026: NSF program page shows 'Status: Waiting for new publication,' no upcoming due dates. Most recent solicitation NSF 23-610 (themes: AI for Astronomical Sciences; AI for Discovery in Materials Research; Strengthening AI). Monitor for a new solicitation.
eligibility: Large multi-institution teams led by US universities/non-profits; specific eligibility set per solicitation. Health/biomedical themes were included in earlier rounds but are NOT in the most recent (NSF 23-610) themes.
description: Program establishing large, multi-institution AI research institutes organized around rotating themes, funding foundational AI plus use-inspired applications.
relevanceToUser: Relevant as a co-PI/collaborator on a large team rather than a solo PI. Prior rounds funded AI-in-health institutes; a future biomedical/health theme would fit AI-for-oncology work. Currently no open call, so track for the next solicitation.

name: Mathematical and Physical Sciences Ascending Postdoctoral Research Fellowships (MPS-Ascend)
funder: National Science Foundation (Directorate for Mathematical and Physical Sciences, MPS)
awardAmount: Historically up to $70,000/year stipend + $30,000/year research/fringe; up to $300,000 over 36 months (per archived NSF 23-501)
deadlineInfo: Uncertain/likely paused: the NSF 23-501 solicitation is archived. Historically third Wednesday in October. Verify whether the program is being offered before relying on it.
eligibility: US citizen, national, or permanent resident; PhD completed before the appointment start; no prior NSF PI/co-PI role. Research must fall within an MPS division (includes DMS/mathematical sciences and statistics). The program was designed to broaden participation of historically underrepresented groups in MPS.
description: Individual postdoctoral fellowship in the mathematical/physical sciences originally aimed at broadening participation, supporting research and transition toward a faculty career.
relevanceToUser: Could fund a statistics/ML postdoc (via DMS) whose methods apply to biomedical/oncology data. However, as a broadening-participation-focused program its current availability is uncertain given recent federal policy changes — confirm it is active before planning around it.

name: Postdoctoral Fellowship (PF)
awardAmount: Progressive stipend of $66,000 / $68,000 / $70,000 per year, plus $4,000/year fellowship allowance (health insurance, meetings) and an extra $1,500 in the final year for a scientific meeting. Total roughly $217,500 over 3 years.
duration: Up to 3 years (parental leave extension available)
deadlineInfo: Two cycles annually: June 1 and December 1. June 1, 2026 has passed; next standard deadline December 1, 2026. Applications open on ProposalCentral ~3 months prior.
eligibility: Must hold a postdoctoral position at a US academic institution or eligible non-profit; terminal doctoral degree for 3 years or less AND 3 or fewer years of postdoctoral research training at the application deadline (clinical-only training not counted). US citizen or non-US citizen with appropriate visa/lawful immigration status. NOT eligible if you hold a faculty appointment (instructor/assistant professor) or are a research/staff scientist. One application per cycle.
description: Mentored fellowship supporting new investigators in research training to position them for independent cancer research careers. ACS explicitly funds AI/computational and in-silico methods across the cancer continuum, so a computational-oncology postdoc project qualifies.
relevanceToUser: The most directly applicable ACS mechanism if the user is a postdoc. Open to any cancer-relevant project including AI/ML applied to blood cancers; no requirement to be wet-lab. Citizenship rules accommodate international postdocs on a valid visa.

name: Research Scholar Grant (RSG)
awardAmount: Up to $215,000 per year in direct costs, plus 10% allowable indirect costs, for up to 4 years (up to $860,000 total direct costs).
deadlineInfo: Two cycles annually: June 1 and December 1. June 1, 2026 has passed; next standard deadline December 1, 2026.
eligibility: Independent, self-directed investigators at a US academic institution or eligible non-profit who were first appointed as independent full-time faculty (or equivalent) 8 or fewer years ago (tightened from prior 10-year window as of Summer 2026). Must be PI on no more than one R01 or R01-equivalent grant at time of application. Prior RSG grantees cannot reapply except via special RFAs. Applicants may submit to only ONE of RSG/CSDG/Mission Boost/Postdoctoral Fellowship per cycle.
description: ACS's flagship independent-investigator award for early-career faculty, supporting investigator-initiated projects across the cancer research continuum within ACS priority areas. AI/ML and computational methodologies are explicitly eligible.
relevanceToUser: The primary ACS grant for an early-career faculty member (e.g., a newly appointed assistant professor at your institution). A computational/AI project on hematologic malignancies fits within ACS priority areas; no citizenship restriction beyond working at a US institution.

name: Clinician Scientist Development Grant (CSDG)
awardAmount: $135,000 per year in direct costs, plus 8% allowable indirect costs. Awards run 3-5 years (up to ~$675,000 over 5 years).
deadlineInfo: Two cycles annually: June 1 and December 1. June 1, 2026 has passed; next standard deadline December 1, 2026.
eligibility: Full-time faculty at a US academic institution or eligible non-profit with a doctoral degree, an active clinical license, and ongoing participation in clinical care, who do not yet lead an independent research program. Cannot be a current/former PI of an individual mentored training grant, nor have been PI of an independent extramural grant exceeding both 1 year and $100k direct costs. Counts against the one-application-per-cycle limit shared with RSG/Mission Boost/PF.
description: Career-development award helping physician-scientists who maintain clinical practice transition to independent cancer investigators. A related ASTRO-ACS CSDG variant exists specifically for radiation oncology.
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the user is (or collaborates as) an MD/clinician-scientist in hematology-oncology; supports computational or translational blood-cancer projects. Not applicable to a non-clinical PhD statistician/ML researcher.

name: Leukemia Exploration and Prevention (LEAP) Grant Program (RFA)
awardAmount: LEAP-Research Scholar Grant: up to $215,000/year for 4 years ($860,000 total direct costs) + 10% indirect. LEAP-Team Award: up to $430,000/year for 4 years ($1,720,000 total direct costs) + 10% indirect. Funding for ~3 LEAP-RSGs, or 1 LEAP-RSG plus 1 LEAP-Team, per cycle.
deadlineInfo: Most recent full-application deadline was June 1, 2026 (LOI typically precedes it); this is a recurring RFA — verify the next cycle on the ACS site. As an RFA the timing can change year to year.
eligibility: Full-time independent investigator at any career stage at a US academic institution or eligible non-profit; some materials indicate the RFA is open to US and international researchers at non-profit academic/research institutions. Projects must target RUNX1-familial platelet disorder (RUNX1-FPD) and associated hematologic malignancies, particularly AML.
description: Blood-cancer-specific RFA funding mechanistic understanding and cancer interception/prevention for RUNX1-FPD hematologic malignancies (notably AML). Investigator-initiated; computational and systems approaches are appropriate.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant to the user's blood-cancer focus and the largest-dollar option here, but scientifically narrow (RUNX1-FPD / AML predisposition). A strong fit for AI/ML work on leukemia interception or genomic risk in this syndrome; less so for general MDS/lymphoma/myeloma.

name: ACS - Flatiron Health Real-World Data Impact Award (RFA)
awardAmount: $100,000 in direct costs for a 1-year award period, plus 10% allowable indirect costs.
deadlineInfo: Rolling/RFA-based; reporting indicates a 60-day window in ProposalCentral once an application is initiated. Verify current cycle on the ACS page (annual award; multiple prior cohorts announced).
eligibility: Independent investigators at any career stage at eligible US academic institutions or non-profits. Notably, applicants are generally expected to be members of the ACS research ecosystem (e.g., current/former ACS grantees or intramural scientists) — confirm current eligibility on the RFA page. Uses Flatiron Health real-world oncology data.
description: Data-science-focused award (ACS + Flatiron Health) supporting research that leverages real-world oncology data to generate clinical evidence.
relevanceToUser: Directly aligned with an AI/ML + real-world-data researcher; ideal if the user works with EHR-derived oncology datasets, including hematologic malignancies. Main caveat is the apparent requirement to already be within the ACS research ecosystem, which may gate a first-time applicant.

name: Institutional Research Grant (IRG) — Pilot / Early-Investigator Awards
funder: American Cancer Society (administered by grantee institutions)
awardAmount: Pilot sub-awards typically $20,000-$60,000 for 1 year (varies by host institution; some fund up to ~$80,000), generally no indirect costs. ACS block grant to the institution is up to ~$120,000/year for 4 years.
duration: 1 year (pilot); institutional block grant runs up to 4 years
deadlineInfo: Set by each host institution's cancer center, not by ACS centrally; usually annual internal calls. Check your institution's cancer center funding office for the current deadline.
eligibility: Junior/early-career faculty (commonly within ~6 years of first independent faculty appointment) who do not currently hold and have not held a national independent research grant such as an R01 (an R00 is typically allowed). Must be at an institution that holds an ACS IRG block grant. Applied for and reviewed locally, not via ProposalCentral.
description: ACS block grants to institutions that redistribute pilot funding to newly independent investigators to generate preliminary data for larger national grants. Computational/data-science pilots are eligible.
relevanceToUser: A realistic entry point for a newly appointed your institution faculty member: small, fast pilot funding to seed an AI/computational-oncology or blood-cancer project. Applied through the local cancer center (verify your institution/affiliate participation), not directly to ACS.

name: Discovery Boost Grant (DBG)
awardAmount: Up to $135,000 per year for up to 2 years, plus 10% indirect costs; maximum allowable budget $297,000 over the 2-year period.
deadlineInfo: PAUSED — ACS is not offering Discovery Boost Grants in the Summer 2026 cycle while it restructures the mechanism. No current deadline; watch for a relaunch announcement.
eligibility: Independent investigators holding a full-time faculty appointment (or equivalent) at a US academic institution or eligible non-profit. (Eligibility as it stood before the pause.)
description: High-risk, high-reward exploratory grant for developing new research methodologies, establishing feasibility, or pilot testing — a natural fit for novel computational/AI methods. Since 2022 it funded 130 projects totaling $38.54M.
relevanceToUser: Would be an excellent fit for high-risk AI/method-development work in oncology, but it is currently suspended for 2026. Track the ACS site for the restructured relaunch; include as a future option rather than a currently open call.

name: ACS - Fanconi Cancer Foundation Discovery Boost Grant (RFA)
funder: American Cancer Society (with Fanconi Cancer Foundation)
awardAmount: Up to $135,000 direct costs per year for up to 2 years, plus 10% indirect costs; maximum allowable budget $297,000.
deadlineInfo: The most recent RFA had a full-application deadline of December 1, 2025 (review Feb-Mar 2026, awards ~Aug 2026, start Oct 1, 2026). That cycle has closed; check the ACS page for whether the RFA is reissued.
eligibility: Independent investigators with a full-time faculty appointment (or equivalent) at a US academic institution or eligible non-profit. Projects must address Fanconi anemia-related cancers / FA-BRCA DNA-repair-defective tumors.
description: Targeted RFA on Fanconi anemia-related cancers, spanning early-detection biomarkers, prevention mechanisms, FA-tailored therapeutics, tumorigenesis models, and systems-biology approaches to find FA-specific vulnerabilities.
relevanceToUser: Relevant to the user's bone-marrow-failure interest: FA is an inherited bone marrow failure syndrome with high MDS/AML risk. Systems-biology and computational approaches are explicitly welcomed, so AI/ML on FA genomics or MDS/AML progression could fit — but the last cycle's deadline has passed and eligibility is limited to independent faculty.

name: Mission Boost Grant (MBG)
awardAmount: Stage I: $135,000/year for up to 2 years, plus 10% indirect ($297,000 total allowable). Stage II: up to $545,000 direct costs (plus 10% indirect) for up to 18 months.
duration: Stage I up to 2 years; Stage II up to 18 months
deadlineInfo: Two cycles annually: June 1 and December 1. June 1, 2026 has passed; next standard deadline December 1, 2026. Counts against the one-application-per-cycle limit with RSG/CSDG/PF.
eligibility: Investigators at any career stage holding a full-time faculty appointment (or equivalent) at eligible US institutions. Stage II requires prior completion of Stage I milestones.
description: Two-stage transitional mechanism for de-risking discoveries and advancing them toward human testing/clinical application.
relevanceToUser: Less aligned with pure methods/AI work because it emphasizes translation to human testing; would fit only if the user's computational tool is being advanced toward a clinical decision-support or diagnostic application in blood cancer. Faculty-only.

name: Ruth L. Kirschstein NRSA Individual Predoctoral Fellowship (Parent F31)
funder: NIH (NCI and most other Institutes/Centers participate)
awardAmount: Stipend ~$29,364/yr (FY2026; FY2025 was $28,788) + tuition/fees allowance (60% of tuition up to $16,000/yr) + institutional allowance $4,750/yr (non-federal/nonprofit). Amounts per current NRSA NOT-OD stipend notice.
duration: Up to 5 years of NRSA support (F31 typically covers the 2-3 year dissertation research phase)
deadlineInfo: Standard fellowship due dates: new applications Apr 8, Aug 8, Dec 8 annually; resubmissions May 8, Sep 8, Jan 8 (AIDS-related dates differ).
eligibility: Must be a U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident at time of award (statutory NRSA requirement; foreign nationals on visas ineligible). Enrolled in a research doctoral (PhD) program and at the dissertation-research stage. Full-time (min 40 hrs/wk) commitment.
description: Predoctoral NRSA fellowship providing mentored dissertation-research support for PhD students. NCI funds F31s with a clear cancer focus regardless of discipline, so an ML/statistics dissertation applied to leukemia/lymphoma/MDS is eligible if framed around NCI's mission.
relevanceToUser: The primary NIH fellowship for a computational/AI PhD student, as long as the dissertation has a defined cancer (e.g., blood-cancer) focus. Builds an independent funding track record early. Note: applicant must be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident.

name: NRSA Individual Predoctoral Fellowship to Promote Diversity in Health-Related Research (Parent F31-Diversity)
awardAmount: Same budget structure as the Parent F31: stipend ~$29,364/yr (FY2026) + tuition/fees (60% up to $16,000/yr) + $4,750/yr institutional allowance.
duration: Up to 5 years of NRSA support
deadlineInfo: Historically the standard fellowship dates (Apr 8 / Aug 8 / Dec 8). CAUTION: current availability is uncertain — NIH restructured/terminated many diversity-designated funding programs in 2025 amid policy changes, and the F31-Diversity NOFO status has been in flux (freezes, appeals, reinstatements). Verify the active NOFO before relying on it.
eligibility: Same as Parent F31 (U.S. citizen/national/permanent resident, enrolled PhD student at dissertation stage) PLUS membership in a group underrepresented in biomedical research: certain racial/ethnic groups, persons with disabilities, or individuals from disadvantaged backgrounds. Requires an institutional diversity-eligibility certification letter.
description: A diversity-focused variant of the F31 with identical funding but an added eligibility layer for underrepresented predoctoral trainees. Same cancer/computational scope as the parent F31.
relevanceToUser: Only relevant if the applicant qualifies under NIH diversity criteria; otherwise use the Parent F31. Its current status is unstable as of 2025-2026, so confirm the NOFO is active before investing effort.

name: NRSA Individual Predoctoral Fellowship for MD/PhD and Dual-Degree Students (Parent F30)
awardAmount: Predoctoral stipend ~$29,364/yr (FY2026) + tuition/fees (dual-degree up to $21,000/yr) + institutional allowance; supports both the PhD research and clinical training phases.
duration: Up to 6 years (at least 50% of award duration devoted to full-time PhD research)
deadlineInfo: Standard fellowship due dates Apr 8 / Aug 8 / Dec 8; resubmissions May 8 / Sep 8 / Jan 8.
eligibility: U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident at time of award. Must be enrolled in an MD/PhD (or other dual clinical-research doctoral, e.g., DO/PhD, DVM/PhD) program and have matriculated no more than 48 months before the application due date, with an identified dissertation project and sponsor. Two parent NOFOs: PA-25-424, and PA-25-425 for students at institutions WITHOUT NIH-funded institutional dual-degree/MSTP programs.
description: The predoctoral fellowship for dual-degree (physician-scientist) trainees, supporting integrated clinical and PhD research training.
relevanceToUser: Relevant ONLY if the researcher is (or is entering) an MD/PhD or other dual-degree program. For a pure computational/statistics PhD, the standard F31 is the correct mechanism; F30 is included for completeness of the physician-scientist path in blood-cancer research.

name: Ruth L. Kirschstein NRSA Individual Postdoctoral Fellowship (Parent F32)
awardAmount: Stipend set by years of postdoc experience (FY2025 NRSA levels: $62,232 at 0 yrs rising to $75,564 at 7+ yrs) + tuition/fees (up to $4,500, or $16,000 if in a degree program) + institutional allowance ~$12,400/yr (non-federal/nonprofit). Per NOT-OD-25-105.
duration: Up to 3 years of postdoctoral NRSA support
deadlineInfo: Standard fellowship due dates Apr 8 / Aug 8 / Dec 8; resubmissions May 8 / Sep 8 / Jan 8.
eligibility: U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident at time of award. Must hold a PhD or equivalent research doctorate (or clinical doctorate) before activation, and identify a sponsor and institution with a suitable training environment.
description: The core individual postdoctoral fellowship. Supports mentored research training toward independence; NCI funds cancer-focused F32s across disciplines including computational/quantitative work.
relevanceToUser: Strong fit for a postdoc doing AI/computational oncology on blood cancers, provided U.S. citizenship or permanent residency. Pairs well as a stepping stone before a K99/R00 or K-award.

name: NIH Pathway to Independence Award (K99/R00)
funder: NIH / NCI (NCI NOFO PAR-25-135; NIH parent PA-24-194)
awardAmount: K99 (mentored) phase: up to ~$100,000/yr salary + fringe + up to $30,000/yr research (NCI). R00 (independent) phase: up to ~$249,000/yr total (salary + research). 8% F&A. Total across both phases up to ~5 years.
duration: K99 phase 1-2 years + R00 phase up to 3 years (up to 5 years combined)
deadlineInfo: K awards use standard dates Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12 (new); check NCI PAR-25-135 for exact cycles. R00 transition request due no later than 2 months before proposed activation.
eligibility: Must be in a mentored postdoctoral position at time of award (independence before the K99 is made voids the award). Postdoc experience limit is normally no more than 4 years at submission (NCI's page lists up to 6 years reflecting temporary extensions — verify the current notice, e.g., NOT-OD-26-021). NOTABLE: no U.S. citizenship requirement for K99 — non-U.S. citizens with appropriate visa status are eligible.
description: Flagship postdoc-to-faculty transition award: a mentored K99 phase followed by an independent R00 phase that activates when the awardee starts a tenure-track (or equivalent) faculty position.
relevanceToUser: Excellent fit for a postdoc in an AI/ML/computational-oncology lab targeting an independent faculty position. A major advantage over F32/NRSA and K25/K01 for this user: K99/R00 has NO citizenship requirement, so international postdocs at your institution can apply. Apply by the 2nd-3rd postdoc year for best benefit.

name: Mentored Quantitative Research Career Development Award (K25)
funder: NIH (NCI, NHLBI, NIBIB, NIGMS and others participate)
awardAmount: Salary support up to the IC-set level (mentored K salary at NCI is up to ~$100,000/yr + fringe, capped by the NIH salary cap ~$227,700 FY2026) + research development support (up to ~$50,000/yr, varies by IC). Exact figures vary by Institute — see NOFO.
deadlineInfo: Standard K due dates Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12 (new); resubmissions Mar 12 / Jul 12 / Nov 12. Parent NOFOs: PA-24-191 (clinical trial not allowed), PA-24-190 (clinical trial required), PA-24-192 (BESH).
eligibility: U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident at time of award. Requires an advanced degree (PhD, MS, etc.) in a QUANTITATIVE field — explicitly including mathematics, statistics, computer science, informatics, physics, and engineering. Candidate is NOT expected to have significant prior biomedical research experience; the award funds the pivot into health/disease research. Min 75% effort. Some ICs add limits (e.g., NIBIB: within 10 years of PhD). Not available to former R01/major-K PIs.
description: A mentored career-development award purpose-built to bring quantitative scientists (stats/CS/ML/engineering) into biomedical research, providing protected time plus mentorship to build biomedical/cancer domain expertise.
relevanceToUser: THE single best-matched mechanism for this user's profile: a statistics/ML researcher from a quantitative lab moving into computational oncology. K25 is designed precisely for applicants whose work has not yet been focused on health/disease, so a limited prior cancer-biology background is an expected feature, not a weakness. Requires U.S. citizenship/permanent residency. NCI or NHLBI would be natural Institutes for blood-cancer work.

name: Mentored Research Scientist Development Award (K01)
awardAmount: Salary up to ~$100,000/yr + fringe (NCI), capped at NIH salary cap; research development support up to ~$50,000/yr; 8% F&A. Varies by IC.
deadlineInfo: Standard K due dates Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12 (new); resubmissions Mar 12 / Jul 12 / Nov 12. Parent NOFOs PA-24-176 (clinical trial not allowed), PA-24-175 (clinical trial required).
eligibility: U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident at time of award (student-visa foreign nationals ineligible). Requires a research or health-professional doctoral degree. Career stage: postdoctoral fellows to non-tenured junior faculty (not yet Associate Professor). Min 75% effort. Not open to former PIs of R01/P01/P50 or other major K awards.
description: A general mentored career-development award for PhD (or clinical-doctorate) scientists needing protected, mentored time to establish an independent research program. Many ICs use K01 for methodological/quantitative and computational skill development.
relevanceToUser: A strong option for a computational PhD as a senior postdoc or new faculty member — especially if the applicant already has some biomedical research background (if not, K25 is the better-tailored choice). Requires U.S. citizenship/permanent residency.

name: Mentored Clinical Scientist Research Career Development Award (K08)
awardAmount: Salary based on effort up to the NIH salary cap + fringe; research development support up to ~$50,000/yr (NCI); 8% F&A.
deadlineInfo: Standard K due dates Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12 (new); resubmissions Mar 12 / Jul 12 / Nov 12. Parent NOFOs PA-24-182 (clinical trial not allowed), PA-24-181 (clinical trial required).
eligibility: U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident at time of award. Requires a CLINICAL doctoral degree (MD, DO, DDS/DMD, DVM, PharmD, OD, ND, DC) or a clinical PhD (e.g., clinical psychology, nursing); typically an active clinical license. Min 75% effort (50% for surgeons). Must not have reached Associate Professor. Not open to former major-award PIs.
description: The mentored career-development award for clinically trained investigators pursuing laboratory or translational (non-patient-oriented) research.
relevanceToUser: Relevant ONLY if the researcher holds a clinical degree. For a computational/statistics PhD without a clinical doctorate, the analogous mechanisms are K25 (best) or K01. Included for completeness of the physician-scientist route into hematologic-malignancy research.

name: Career Transition Award (K22) — e.g., NCI Transition Career Development Award
funder: NIH (NCI historically; some other ICs still offer K22)
awardAmount: (NCI historic terms) Salary up to $100,000/yr + fringe + up to $50,000/yr research development; 8% F&A; 3 years.
deadlineInfo: IMPORTANT: As of November 13, 2025, NCI is NO LONGER accepting K22 applications. The K22 activity code still exists and some other Institutes may offer their own K22 NOFOs — verify per-IC availability. NCI historically used Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12 cycles.
eligibility: U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, or permanent resident. Research or clinical doctorate. At least 2 but no more than 8 years of mentored, non-independent research experience after the doctorate; transitioning to a first independent (tenure-track or equivalent) faculty position. Min 75% effort. Cannot already hold/have held a tenure-track faculty post or major individual K award.
description: A career-transition award designed to carry a senior postdoc across the gap into a first independent faculty position (unlike mentored K awards, the K22 largely supports the independent phase).
relevanceToUser: Was a route to a first faculty appointment in cancer research, but NCI closed it to new applications in Nov 2025. A computational-oncology applicant should treat K99/R00 as the primary transition award and check whether NHLBI or other blood-relevant ICs still run a K22.

name: NIH Director's Early Independence Award (DP5)
funder: NIH Common Fund (High-Risk, High-Reward Research program)
awardAmount: Up to $250,000 in direct costs per year (plus applicable F&A); grants.gov listing cites up to ~$350,000 total/yr and ~10-13 awards per cycle.
deadlineInfo: Current cycle RFA-RM-27-004: application due September 10, 2026 (reference letters Aug 10 - Sep 10, 2026). Common Fund runs this annually.
eligibility: U.S. domestic institution. The PD/PI must complete their terminal research doctorate (or postgraduate clinical training) within a narrow window around the award — for this cycle, between about May 1, 2025 and Sept 30, 2027 (roughly 12 months before to 16 months after the degree). Must have a guaranteed or pending independent research position and be able to start independent research at the project start; the mechanism lets exceptional graduates SKIP a postdoc.
description: A high-risk/high-reward award that lets an exceptional new PhD launch an independent lab immediately after graduate school, bypassing the traditional postdoc.
relevanceToUser: Well suited to a standout your institution computational/AI PhD who wants to start an independent AI+oncology research program right away rather than doing a postdoc. Highly competitive (~10-13 awards/yr NIH-wide) and requires an institution willing to host the new PI. No stated citizenship restriction, but eligibility hinges on the tight degree-completion window.

name: Ruth L. Kirschstein NRSA Institutional Research Training Grant (T32)
funder: NIH (awarded to institutions; NCI, NHLBI, NLM/NHGRI fund cancer- and data-science-relevant T32s)
awardAmount: Trainees receive standard NRSA stipends (predoc ~$29,364/yr FY2026; postdoc $62,232-$75,564/yr by experience, FY2025 levels) plus tuition and training-related expenses paid through the training program.
duration: Individual appointments typically 1-2 years (cumulative NRSA support limits: up to 5 yrs predoc, up to 3 yrs postdoc)
deadlineInfo: Institution (not the individual) applies to the Parent T32 (PA-25-168), commonly on the May 25 / Sep 25 / Jan 25 cycle. Individuals join an existing funded program via their department.
eligibility: Trainees must be U.S. citizens, non-citizen nationals, or permanent residents at appointment (statutory NRSA requirement). Must be enrolled in a research doctoral program or in postdoctoral training at the institution holding the T32. The grant itself is applied for by the institution/program director.
description: Institutional training grants that fund cohorts of predoctoral and postdoctoral trainees in a defined area. An individual does not apply directly to NIH — they get appointed to a slot on a program their institution already holds.
relevanceToUser: A practical funding route for a U.S. citizen/permanent-resident PhD student or postdoc: look for T32 programs at your institution/Harvard/Broad in bioinformatics, computational biology, data science, cancer biology, or hematology and ask to be appointed. Provides NRSA stipend support plus structured training. Not open to international trainees.

name: Career Development Program (CDP) - Fellow
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: $70,000/year (minimum $65,000 stipend paid directly to awardee) for 3 years = up to $210,000; plus up to $3,600/year optional childcare stipend
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2025-2026: eligibility check due Oct 17, 2025; Letter of Intent Nov 1, 2025; full application + reference letters Nov 15, 2025
eligibility: Early-stage postdoc with 3 years or less of postdoctoral research training by the award start date; must hold PhD, MD, DVM or equivalent; mentored (non-independent) position; open to US citizens and non-citizens at non-profit institutions worldwide; research must be directly relevant to hematologic malignancies/pre-malignant conditions (graduate training need not have been in blood cancer)
description: Stipend/salary support for early postdoctoral researchers to pursue mentored research relevant to blood cancers, building toward research independence.
relevanceToUser: This is the earliest-career LLS mechanism a computational/ML researcher could hold: an ML/comp-bio postdoc (within 3 years of the PhD) working on AI applied to leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS qualifies, since research need only be 'relevant to' hematologic malignancies. Note LLS has no dedicated PhD-student or dedicated AI/data-science track, so this Fellow tier is the entry point for trainees. A pure-methods PhD would need a blood-cancer-focused project and typically a hematology-connected mentor.

name: Career Development Program (CDP) - Special Fellow
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: $75,000/year (minimum $70,000 stipend paid directly to awardee) for up to 3 years = up to approximately $225,000; plus up to $3,600/year optional childcare stipend
duration: Up to 3 years (2-3 years depending on eligibility)
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2025-2026: eligibility check due Oct 17, 2025; Letter of Intent Nov 1, 2025; full application + reference letters Nov 15, 2025
eligibility: More advanced postdoc/instructor (roughly 3-5 years of postdoctoral research training) who has demonstrated success and needs 2-3 more years of training to compete for an independent position; PhD, MD, DVM or equivalent; still in a mentored position; non-profit institutions worldwide; research directly relevant to hematologic malignancies
description: Higher-level stipend support for senior postdocs/instructors nearing research independence, working on blood cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Best fit for a senior computational-oncology postdoc/instructor (3-5 years out) about to go on the faculty market. A machine-learning-for-blood-cancer project qualifies. Slightly higher stipend than the Fellow tier and aimed at those closest to independence.

name: Career Development Program (CDP) - Scholar
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: Approximately $120,000/year for 5 years = up to approximately $600,000
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2025-2026: eligibility check due Oct 17, 2025; Letter of Intent Nov 1, 2025; full application Nov 15, 2025
eligibility: Independent, tenure-track (or equivalent) faculty investigator with substantial national research support who has not yet reached full professor (full professors are ineligible); PhD, MD, DVM or equivalent; non-profit institutions worldwide. Laboratory-based and computational/model-system blood cancer research is eligible (unlike Scholar in Clinical Research, patient involvement is NOT required)
description: Five-year salary/research support for established-but-not-senior independent faculty pursuing blood cancer research.
relevanceToUser: The strongest LLS fit for a newly independent computational/AI faculty member (e.g., a stats/ML PI): the Scholar tier explicitly accommodates lab-based and model-system (i.e., non-patient, computational) blood cancer research, so AI/ML methods work on leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS/bone-marrow-failure data qualifies. Five years of flexible support is unusual and valuable for method-development programs.

name: Career Development Program (CDP) - Scholar in Clinical Research
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: $125,000/year for 5 years = up to approximately $625,000
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2025-2026: eligibility check due Oct 17, 2025; Letter of Intent Nov 1, 2025; full application Nov 15, 2025
eligibility: Independent, faculty-level investigator conducting independent clinical research directly involving patients (often early-phase trials) to advance prevention/diagnosis/treatment of blood cancers; appointment to an independent tenure-track position generally required (recent cycles referenced appointments on/after ~Jan 1, 2006); full professors ineligible. Primarily lab-based or model-system researchers should apply to the Scholar tier instead
description: Five-year support for early/mid-career clinical investigators leading patient-based blood cancer research such as early-stage trials.
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the user is (or partners with) a clinician-scientist doing patient-facing blood cancer trials. For a pure computational/ML researcher this is the wrong tier - the equivalent lab/computational track is the Scholar award. Worth knowing as the mechanism a clinical collaborator on an AI project would use.

name: Translational Research Program (TRP)
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: Up to $250,000/year (inclusive of up to 10% indirect costs) for 3 years = up to $750,000
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2025-2026: Letter of Intent due Oct 16, 2025 (3:00 pm ET); full application by invitation Jan 21, 2026 (3:00 pm ET); project period typically starts ~July 1
eligibility: Independent investigator with dedicated lab space who hires/supervises personnel, with 3+ years in an independent faculty position and a significant track record in blood cancer research (or must add a Co-PI with that expertise); PhD, MD, DVM or equivalent; non-profit academic institutions worldwide; NIH/government-agency PIs not eligible. Predoctoral students and postdocs are NOT eligible. Requires proof-of-concept/preliminary data (e.g., in vivo evidence)
description: Larger multi-year grant to move promising laboratory findings toward clinical application (bench-to-bedside) in hematologic malignancies.
relevanceToUser: Fits an established computational-oncology PI whose AI/ML method has matured to a translational stage (e.g., a validated predictive/diagnostic model or a computationally-nominated therapeutic target with supporting proof-of-concept data). Requires a blood cancer research track record or a Co-PI who has one, so pairing with a hematology lab strengthens competitiveness. Larger budget than the CDP tiers.

name: Discovery Grant Program (DGP) - formerly Blood Cancer Discoveries Grant (BCDG)
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: Up to $250,000/year for 3 years = up to $750,000 (per BCDG program guidelines; verify current-cycle figure on the LLS portal)
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. FY26: Letter of Intent due Aug 19, 2025 (3:00 pm ET); full application by invitation later in the cycle (see LLS Research Portal for exact full-app date)
eligibility: Established/independent investigator, defined as more than 3 years in an independent faculty appointment at the time of the Letter of Intent; PhD, MD, DVM or equivalent; non-profit academic institutions; open worldwide
description: Supports cutting-edge, discovery-oriented basic research aimed at understanding blood cancer properties and vulnerabilities, including work using genomics and other modern tools.
relevanceToUser: Good fit for an established PI doing discovery-stage computational/AI work on blood cancer biology - e.g., ML on genomic/single-cell/multi-omic leukemia or lymphoma datasets to uncover novel vulnerabilities. The program explicitly embraces genomics-era tools, which covers data-science/computational approaches. Requires >3 years as independent faculty, so not for trainees.

name: Specialized Center of Research Program (SCOR)
funder: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS) / Blood Cancer United
awardAmount: Up to $1,000,000/year (up to $850,000 direct + up to $150,000 indirect, or ~15% indirects) for 5 years = up to $5,000,000 total
deadlineInfo: Periodic cycle. Most recent cycle: Letter of Intent ~Dec 6, 2024; full application ~Mar 31, 2025. Check the LLS/Blood Cancer United site for the current cycle's dates (not always offered annually)
eligibility: Team of established investigators from one or several institutions, organized as a Program Leader plus 3-5 scientific Projects and 2-4 Cores (including an administrative Core); each PI must hold PhD, MD, DVM or equivalent at a non-profit institution; open worldwide. Emphasis on integrated basic + translational work leading toward clinical application
description: Large team-science center grant uniting multiple established investigators around a focused, interdisciplinary blood cancer research program spanning basic to translational science.
relevanceToUser: Not an individual mechanism, but a computational/AI lab is a natural component of a SCOR - a stats/ML group can lead a Project or a data-science/computational Core within a multi-PI center on a leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS theme. Realistic path for the user is to join an existing or forming SCOR team at a partner cancer center rather than lead one solo.

name: ASH Scholar Award (Fellow, Fellow-to-Faculty, and Junior Faculty levels)
awardAmount: Fellow level: up to $100,000 total (max $50,000/yr); Fellow-to-Faculty level: up to $125,000 total; Junior Faculty level: up to $150,000 total. All paid over a 2-3 year period in equal quarterly installments.
deadlineInfo: 2027-activation cycle: LOI due May 1, 2026; full application due Aug 3, 2026 (11:59pm ET); notification Jan 2027; activation July 2027. Annual cycle.
eligibility: ASH member in good standing at LOI deadline; U.S. or Canadian citizen (or holds a visa/permanent-resident status and will conduct research in the U.S. or Canada); holds MD, DO, MD-PhD, PhD, or PharmD. Years-from-degree window sets the level: for PhD/PharmD, Fellow = <6 yrs post-degree (3-5 yrs research experience), Fellow-to-Faculty = 6-11 yrs, Junior Faculty = 11-13 yrs (or any independent faculty appointment). Historically required >=75% protected research time; for 2027 cycle applicants with <75% may now apply with justification.
description: ASH's flagship career-transition award providing partial salary/research support to hematologists moving from training to independent investigator status. The Fellow-to-Faculty level specifically targets the fellowship-to-faculty transition. Proposals accepted across basic, translational, patient-oriented clinical, and outcomes-based hematology research, with strong emphasis on blood cancers.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant. PhD holders (not just MDs) are eligible, so a computational/ML postdoc or early-career faculty member working on leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS can apply; computational, translational, and outcomes projects all qualify. The Fellow and Fellow-to-Faculty levels fit a postdoc-to-faculty transition; Junior Faculty fits an assistant professor. Requires ASH membership and a hematologic (not solid-tumor) focus.

name: ASH Research Training Award for Fellows (RTAF)
awardAmount: $70,000 for a one-year period (protected research time), plus $1,000 the following year for ASH Annual Meeting attendance. Up to four recipients per year may apply for a second year of funding.
duration: 1 year (possible second year for up to 4 recipients)
deadlineInfo: 2026-activation cycle: application opened Sept 1, 2025, closed Jan 15, 2026; finalist interviews Mar 30-31, 2026; notification May 2026. Next cycle expected to close ~January 2027 (start July 2027). Annual.
eligibility: Must be a fellow in a hematology, hematology/oncology, or hematology-related training program in the U.S. or Canada, and an ASH Associate member (or application pending). Must hold an MD, DO, DO-PhD, or MD-PhD and be 0-4 years from completing residency at the deadline. Requires a mentor with active federal/foundation funding for the award term. Project must focus on a hematologic condition (solid-tumor projects ineligible).
description: Provides protected research time to hematology/heme-onc clinical fellows to launch an investigative career in blood-disease research (basic, translational, clinical, or outcomes).
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the user is a physician-scientist: this mechanism requires an MD/DO/MD-PhD and enrollment in a clinical hematology fellowship. A pure computational PhD (no clinical fellowship) would not qualify. For an MD-PhD doing AI/computational work in blood cancers, it is an excellent early-career fit and the project may be computational.

name: ASH-Amos Medical Faculty Development Program (ASH-AMFDP)
funder: American Society of Hematology (ASH) in partnership with the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation / Harold Amos Medical Faculty Development Program
awardAmount: Total of $420,000 over four years: annual stipend of up to $75,000 plus an annual $30,000 research grant, along with a National Advisory Committee mentor, complimentary ASH membership, and covered travel to the ASH and AMFDP annual meetings.
deadlineInfo: 2026-activation cycle: application available Aug 15, 2025; deadline Oct 23, 2025; interviews and notification Jan 2026; activation 2026. Annual (next cycle expected ~fall 2026). Verify current dates on site.
eligibility: U.S. citizens or permanent residents from historically marginalized/underrepresented backgrounds in hematology. Must hold an MD (or equivalent) OR a PhD working in a medical setting, be a postdoctoral fellow/junior faculty developing an academic research career, and dedicate at least 70% of time to research. Projects in basic/biomedical, clinical investigation, or health-services/epidemiology hematology research.
description: A four-year faculty-development award to increase the number of hematology faculty from historically underrepresented/marginalized backgrounds, providing substantial salary and research support during the postdoc-to-faculty transition.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user is from an underrepresented background and is a U.S. citizen/permanent resident. Notably open to PhDs working in a medical setting (not only MDs), so a computational scientist embedded in a medical/clinical research environment studying blood cancers could qualify. Among the most generous ASH mechanisms ($420K).

name: ASH Bridge Grant
awardAmount: $150,000 paid in a single installment.
deadlineInfo: Shares its application deadline with the ASH Scholar Award; winners notified in July. Eligibility was significantly expanded in 2025. Verify exact 2026 dates on the ASH site. Annual/recurring.
eligibility: ASH member in good standing (maintained for the award term); submitted a hematology-related R01 or R01-equivalent to NIH within the past 18 months (as of deadline), including first R01 submissions and scored Type 2 renewals; must be PI or co-PI; must hold a faculty position at an academic institution or nonprofit research organization; at activation, no more than $500,000/yr in other research funding (excluding salary/fringe, internal funds, and donated trial drugs).
description: Provides bridge funding to sustain a hematology research program (malignant or classical) when a scored NIH R01/equivalent was not funded, covering basic, translational, clinical, or outcomes research.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant for an early-career or established faculty PI whose blood-cancer / computational-oncology R01 was scored but not funded. Open to any hematology research including AI/computational and data-science methods projects. Requires an existing NIH R01 application and a faculty PI position.

name: ASH Award for Research Careers in Hematology (ARCH)
awardAmount: $150,000 research award; funding period selectable by the recipient between 1 and 3 years.
deadlineInfo: Recipients for the current cycle to be notified mid-2026; exact application deadline not stated on the key-information page - see site. Annual.
eligibility: ASH member in good standing (maintained for the award term); holds PharmD, PhD, MD/DO, or MD/DO-PhD (or equivalent); must have completed terminal doctoral degree (PhD/PharmD) or residency (MD/DO) MORE than 13 years prior to the deadline (i.e., mid-career/established). Must be a U.S., Mexican, or Canadian citizen conducting research anywhere, OR hold a visa/permanent-resident status in the U.S., Mexico, or Canada and conduct research there. Basic, translational, patient-oriented clinical, or outcomes research.
description: A newer independent research award designed to make hematology research more accessible for mid-career and established investigators (more than 13 years past terminal degree/residency).
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user is an established investigator (>13 years post-PhD) running a lab in computational oncology / blood cancers. PhDs are eligible and computational/outcomes projects qualify. Not appropriate for students, postdocs, or newly independent faculty (those fit the Scholar Award instead).

name: ASH Graduate Hematology Award
awardAmount: $40,000 annual stipend for a two-year period (~$80,000 total); usable for tuition/salary/stipend (no more than 70%), research, training-related expenses (incl. health insurance), and ASH Annual Meeting travel.
deadlineInfo: 2026-cycle: LOI opened Aug 15, 2025, closed Nov 3, 2025; full application available Dec 15, 2025, deadline Feb 2, 2026; notification May 2026. Annual (next LOI window expected ~Aug-Nov 2026).
eligibility: Enrolled in a PhD-granting graduate program at a U.S. or Canadian institution, within the first three years of the PhD (i.e., with about two years remaining at the award start date). Must plan an investigative career in hematology, propose research focused on a hematologic condition or biological process, have an ASH-member mentor in good standing, and provide department-chair support for protected research time.
description: A two-year stipend award encouraging early-stage PhD students to pursue academic careers in hematology research.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant for a PhD student in a statistics/ML lab in their first ~3 years, provided the dissertation targets a hematologic condition or biological process (e.g., AI/ML methods for leukemia, lymphoma, myeloma, MDS, or bone-marrow failure). Requires an ASH-member mentor. One of the few ASH mechanisms open to graduate students.

name: ASH Global Research Award
awardAmount: Up to $100,000 for projects of one year or less; up to $150,000 for projects longer than one year. Funds projects up to three years.
deadlineInfo: Accepting Letters of Intent until Aug 31, 2026 (11:59pm ET). Annual.
eligibility: For emerging hematology researchers based OUTSIDE the U.S. and Canada, between completion of training and establishment of an independent career. Must be an ASH member in good standing and hold an MD, PhD, MD-PhD, or equivalent degree.
description: Supports independent projects of early-career hematology researchers outside the U.S. and Canada to foster global research collaboration.
relevanceToUser: Low relevance / likely ineligible: this award is explicitly restricted to researchers BASED OUTSIDE the U.S. and Canada. A U.S.-based your institution researcher would not qualify. Included for completeness of the ASH cluster; the U.S.-eligible analogue is the Scholar Award.

name: Emerging Leader Award
funder: The Mark Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $1,000,000 total ($250,000/year for 4 years; indirects capped at 10% of direct costs, included in total). New for the 2026 application cycle onward (previously $750,000).
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2026 cycle: LOI due April 30, 2026 (5pm ET); full application July 30, 2026; grant start Jan 1, 2027. Expect a comparable ~April LOI / ~July full-app window in 2027.
eligibility: MD, PhD or equivalent who established an independent lab 3-8 years before Dec 31 of the award year (2026 cycle: labs founded 2018-2023). Must have dedicated lab space, ability to hire, PI grant authority, and multi-year independent funding active in the grant year. No geography or citizenship restrictions (worldwide).
description: Flagship early-career award funding high-impact, high-risk cancer projects distinct from the applicant's current portfolio. The Mark Foundation is one of the most computational/AI-friendly cancer funders, regularly backing data-science and machine-learning oncology.
relevanceToUser: Direct fit for an early-career faculty (once independent, 3-8 years) doing AI/ML in computational oncology; the foundation explicitly rewards high-risk, technology-forward cancer projects, and blood-cancer computational work is in scope.

name: ASPIRE Award (ASPIRE I and ASPIRE II)
funder: The Mark Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: ASPIRE I: up to $250,000 (up to $500,000 for clinical studies). ASPIRE II: up to $750,000. Indirects not to exceed 10% of direct costs.
duration: ASPIRE I: 12 months or less (up to 24 months for clinical). ASPIRE II: 36 months or less.
deadlineInfo: No fixed annual deadline; awarded through focused RFPs or by invitation. See site / join mailing list for open RFPs.
eligibility: PI must be an independent researcher at their institution. Worldwide; collaborations and co-investigators permitted.
description: Accelerating Scientific Platforms and Innovative Research awards fund innovative, high-impact ideas outside the scope of conventional funding, explicitly including an 'Innovative Technologies' domain that integrates new technology with cancer biology.
relevanceToUser: The 'Innovative Technologies' and basic-research tracks are well suited to novel AI/ML methods and computational platforms applied to cancer, including hematologic malignancies. Watch for topic-specific RFPs.

name: Endeavor Award
funder: The Mark Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $3,000,000 total per team.
deadlineInfo: Cycle-based; next cycle to be announced ('Stay tuned'). See site.
eligibility: Team of one PI plus 2-8 co-PIs (co-PIs get min 10% of budget), each holding an independent (tenure-track or equivalent) faculty appointment at a nonprofit academic/research institution. No citizenship/geography restrictions. Cannot hold concurrent funding for the same work. Any cancer type.
description: Large team-science awards for multidisciplinary collaborations addressing an urgent scientific question in cancer prevention, diagnosis, or treatment where the combined effort exceeds the sum of parts.
relevanceToUser: A strong vehicle for a computational/ML lab to join a multi-PI team (e.g. pairing statistical/ML methods with experimental hematology-oncology groups on leukemia/lymphoma/MDS). Best pursued as a co-PI bringing the AI/data-science component.

name: Momentum and Postdoctoral Fellowships
funder: The Mark Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: Not published (varies). Momentum fellows are funded for ~1 year; partner postdoctoral fellowships (e.g. EACR-Mark) run up to 3 years.
deadlineInfo: See site. Momentum Fellowship is by nomination only (Scientific Advisory Committee); postdoctoral fellowships are administered through partner organizations, not directly by the Mark Foundation.
eligibility: Momentum: nomination-only, no direct application accepted. Postdoctoral: apply through partner organizations' mechanisms (note the flagship EACR-Mark partner fellowship is Europe-based).
description: Fellowship programs supporting promising cancer researchers; not a direct-application route for most US postdocs.
relevanceToUser: Lower direct applicability: Momentum is nomination-only and the main postdoc partner (EACR) is European. Worth knowing about if a US partner-org fellowship opens, but not an application the user can submit unsolicited today.

name: Break Through Cancer TeamLabs (incl. AML TeamLab and Data Science Hub)
awardAmount: Varies; funding flows to institutional teams (initial commitment ~$50M across projects). No published per-investigator open-application amount.
deadlineInfo: No open public application. Projects are assembled by the foundation with its five member institutions. Engage via your institution. See site / contact info@breakthroughcancer.org.
eligibility: Research is conducted through the five member institutions: Dana-Farber, Johns Hopkins (Sidney Kimmel), Memorial Sloan Kettering, MIT (Koch Institute), and MD Anderson. Not an open individual-applicant grant.
description: Radical-collaboration 'TeamLab' model funding cross-institution teams. Includes an Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) TeamLab (blood cancer) and a dedicated Data Science Hub applying generative-AI digital pathology, spatial transcriptomics, and computational diagnostics.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant thematically (AML + Data Science Hub) and the user is at your institution, a member institution (Koch Institute) - so participation is realistically accessible through internal your institution/Koch channels rather than an open call. Combines blood cancer and AI/computational focus.

name: Accelerating and Scaling Biological Sciences with AI (GPU Compute RFA)
awardAmount: In-kind GPU allocation (minimum 96 GPUs) on CZI's 1,024x NVIDIA H100 DGX SuperPOD cluster. No cash award.
deadlineInfo: Most recent cycle ran rolling Jan 9-Jun 18, 2025 and is now closed (cluster can fully allocate early). Recurring/rolling - monitor the RFA page for the next window.
eligibility: PIs and Co-PIs must hold faculty or equivalent independent-investigator status at US-based nonprofit institutions (universities, hospitals, independent labs, government). For-profit entities ineligible.
description: Provides large-scale GPU compute (not obtainable with typical university resources) to train foundation models for biology, aligned with CZI's Virtual Cell initiative.
relevanceToUser: Ideal for an ML lab building large biological foundation models - directly supports training compute for AI-on-cells / virtual-cell work that could be applied to hematopoietic single-cell and cancer datasets. US-faculty eligibility fits an your institution PI.

name: Single-Cell Biology Data Insights RFA
awardAmount: Focused projects: $200,000 total costs; Expanded projects: $400,000 total costs (each inclusive of up to 15% indirects). ~$14.9M across three cycles.
deadlineInfo: Ran in three cycles; Cycle 3 closed (Dec 2023, awards started Aug 2024) and was described as the last currently planned cycle. Currently closed - monitor CZI Science funding page for any successor RFA.
eligibility: PIs at domestic or foreign nonprofit institutions (up to 3 PIs per application). Early-career investigators (0-6 years independent) strongly encouraged; computational experts from outside single-cell biology explicitly welcomed.
description: Funds computational methods, meta-analysis, and tools that extract biological insight from large single-cell datasets, including AI/ML approaches and multimodal/multiscale integration.
relevanceToUser: Squarely in the user's wheelhouse - ML/statistical methods for single-cell data, applicable to leukemia/lymphoma/MDS and bone-marrow single-cell atlases. Even if the current series has closed, it signals CZI's continuing appetite for exactly this profile; watch for the next RFA.

name: AACR Hematologic Malignancies Research Fellowship
awardAmount: $130,000 total over 2 years (supports fellow salary/benefits; funds may also cover supplies, equipment, publication costs).
deadlineInfo: Annual, roughly January (2025 cycle deadline was Jan 21, 2025). Expect a comparable ~January 2026/2027 deadline - see site.
eligibility: Postdoctoral or clinical research fellow within 5 years of most recent doctoral degree. Must be an AACR member with legal right to work in the research country. Not open to US-government or for-profit-industry employees/subcontractors. Basic, translational, clinical, or population research on hematologic malignancies.
description: AACR's first fellowship dedicated exclusively to blood cancers, for mentored hematologic-malignancy research across any discipline.
relevanceToUser: The single best-targeted item for a blood-cancer researcher at the postdoc/clinical-fellow stage - directly funds leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS work, and computational/AI methodology projects qualify under basic or translational research.

name: AACR Anna D. Barker Basic Cancer Research Fellowship
awardAmount: $110,000 total over 2 years.
deadlineInfo: Annual; competitive LOI historically due ~October. See site for current-cycle dates.
eligibility: Postdoctoral or clinical research fellow conducting mentored basic cancer research; AACR membership required.
description: Postdoctoral fellowship supporting basic cancer research; basic/quantitative and computational-biology projects are eligible.
relevanceToUser: A general postdoc mechanism that accommodates computational/ML basic-science projects in cancer (including hematologic malignancy biology) when framed as basic research.

name: AACR Trailblazer Cancer Research Grants (Early-Stage and Mid-Career tracks)
awardAmount: $1,000,000 each over 3 years (AACR's largest-ever grant program; ~$15M total across 9 early-stage + 6 mid-career awards).
deadlineInfo: Annual. Launched ~April 2025; awards announced for 2026. Check site for current LOI/full-application dates.
eligibility: Early-Stage track: first independent assistant-professor appointment within the past 6 years (with confirmed independent lab space), at an academic/medical/research institution anywhere in the world. Mid-Career track: active AACR member, tenured associate professor, first NIH R01 (or equivalent) within the past 10 years.
description: Flagship large-dollar independent-investigator grants to launch or accelerate innovative, emerging cancer-research programs at two distinct career stages.
relevanceToUser: Substantial, discipline-agnostic funding for an independent computational-oncology faculty member; AI/ML methods applied to blood cancers fit the 'innovative, emerging' framing. Two tracks cover both newly independent and mid-career PIs.

name: AACR NextGen Grants for Transformative Cancer Research
awardAmount: $450,000 total costs over 3 years.
deadlineInfo: Annual (recent cycle grant start July 1, 2026). LOI typically fall, full application ~winter/spring. See site.
eligibility: At grant start, applicant must hold a tenure-track (or equivalent) position at the rank of assistant professor. Intended for junior faculty pursuing transformative, high-risk/high-reward research. (Several sponsor-specific NextGen variants also exist, e.g. BCRF, TNBC Foundation, Fanconi Cancer Foundation.)
description: Career-development grants for junior faculty pursuing transformative cancer research; high-risk methodological and computational projects are well suited.
relevanceToUser: A good early-faculty vehicle for transformative AI/ML-in-oncology proposals; the 'transformative' framing rewards novel computational methodology, and disease scope is open (blood cancers eligible).

name: AACR-Novocure Career Development Award for Cancer Research
funder: American Association for Cancer Research (AACR) / Novocure
awardAmount: See site (amount not verified on official page at time of review).
deadlineInfo: See site for current cycle.
eligibility: Early-career investigators conducting innovative research focused on Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields). Computational/mechanistic-modeling approaches are explicitly welcomed (e.g. prior grantees combined mechanistic computational modeling with imaging data).
description: Career-development award supporting mechanism-of-action research on Tumor Treating Fields, with a demonstrated track record of funding hybrid experimental-computational and imaging-driven modeling projects.
