relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user's AI/computational-modeling interests extend to therapy-response modeling; note the topic is restricted to TTFields (solid-tumor modality) rather than blood cancers, so it is a fit for methods more than for the hematologic-malignancy focus.

name: AACR-AstraZeneca Career Development Award for Physician-Scientists (in Honor of Jose Baselga)
funder: American Association for Cancer Research (AACR) / AstraZeneca
awardAmount: See site (amount not verified on official page at time of review).
deadlineInfo: See site for current cycle.
eligibility: Physician-scientists in early-career development; AACR membership expected. (Confirm current eligibility windows on the AACR career-development-awards page.)
description: Career-development award specifically for physician-scientists conducting cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Included to cover the physician-scientist career stage noted in the request; disease scope is open, so a clinician-scientist doing computational/AI hematologic-oncology research could apply. Verify amount and eligibility on the official page.

name: Hanna H. Gray Fellows Program
awardAmount: Up to $1.5M total over up to 8 years. Postdoc phase: up to 2 years of salary/stipend ($80,000–$104,382/yr) plus $50,000/yr for expenses; support continues into the early faculty phase.
duration: Up to 8 years (postdoctoral phase into independent faculty phase)
deadlineInfo: Annual. ARC portal opens Nov 3, 2026; closes Dec 15, 2026 (11:59pm ET). Beginning with the 2028 competition it becomes a single combined competition with the Freeman Hrabowski Scholars Program.
eligibility: Postdoctoral researchers with no more than 7 years of postdoctoral experience (as of Dec 15, 2026) at an HHMI-eligible U.S. institution. Field-agnostic across basic biomedical research; open to all backgrounds. Fellows who land a qualifying tenure-track faculty post are appointed Freeman Hrabowski Scholars.
description: A postdoc-to-faculty transition award providing salary, expense support, and tailored professional development (including faculty-search help) to early-career scientists across all areas of basic biomedical research.
relevanceToUser: Field-agnostic, so an AI/ML-for-oncology or computational blood-cancer project qualifies. One of the most prestigious postdoc-to-faculty bridges; ideal if the user is a postdoc aiming to launch an independent computational-oncology lab.

name: Freeman Hrabowski Scholars Program
awardAmount: Up to $8.6M over 10 years: full salary and benefits plus a research budget of ~$400,000/yr in years 1–5 and ~$600,000/yr in years 6–10.
duration: 5 years, renewable for a second 5-year term (up to 10 years)
deadlineInfo: Annual. Combined competition with Hanna Gray Fellows from the 2028 cycle: ARC opens Nov 3, 2026; closes Dec 15, 2026 (11:59pm ET).
eligibility: Early-career faculty who have led an independent lab for no more than 7 years (as of Dec 15, 2026) at an HHMI-eligible host institution. Any area of basic biomedical research; emphasis on scientific excellence and inclusive lab leadership.
description: A long-horizon (up to 10-year, up to $8.6M) appointment supporting outstanding early-career faculty as full HHMI-style investigators, funding people rather than specific projects.
relevanceToUser: Directly applicable for a new your institution-style computational-oncology faculty PI within their first 7 years of running a lab; field-agnostic so AI/ML + blood-cancer work is fully in scope. This is the highest-dollar mechanism in this cluster.

name: LSRF Postdoctoral Fellowship
awardAmount: $85,000/yr ($79,000 stipend/salary + $6,000 research allowance); $255,000 total over 3 years. Paid to the host institution.
deadlineInfo: Annual. Application due Oct 1, 2026 (8:00pm ET); three reference letters + one proposed-supervisor letter due Nov 1, 2026 (8:00pm ET). Awards activate Aug 1.
eligibility: Within 5 years of PhD (or MD/DVM, whichever is later) as of Oct 1, 2026. Postdoc lab must differ from the thesis lab; research must be at a nonprofit institution. U.S. citizens may work anywhere; foreign citizens must work in U.S. labs. Cannot hold a concurrent postdoc fellowship.
description: A broad, field-agnostic postdoctoral fellowship across all of the life sciences, sponsor-mentored, with an annual spring meeting fellows attend.
relevanceToUser: Covers computational biology and cancer research with no field restriction, so an AI/ML-driven blood-cancer postdoc project qualifies. Competitive, prestigious, and non-citizen-friendly for U.S.-based labs.

name: Jane Coffin Childs Postdoctoral Fellowship
funder: Jane Coffin Childs Fund for Medical Research
awardAmount: Stipend Yr1 $72,000 / Yr2 $74,000 / Yr3 $76,000; plus $2,000/yr lab research allowance, $1,000/yr per dependent child under 18, and $1,800 one-time meeting-travel allowance.
deadlineInfo: Annual. Application deadline Feb 1 (Feb 1, 2027 for the next cycle; 3:00pm EST). If awarded, must start in the lab before July 1.
eligibility: PhD conferred no more than 24 months before the deadline (or MD no more than 3 years); no more than 12 months of postdoc experience at deadline; any nationality (non-U.S. citizens must hold the fellowship at a U.S. institution); may not train in the same lab where the PhD was earned.
description: A long-standing postdoctoral fellowship rooted in cancer/medical research, funding early postdocs across biomedical fields including computational approaches to disease.
relevanceToUser: The Fund's cancer/medical-research mission fits blood-cancer biology, and computational/quantitative projects are eligible. Strong option for an early-stage postdoc (first year) including non-U.S. citizens at U.S. institutions.

name: Helen Hay Whitney Foundation Postdoctoral Research Fellowship
awardAmount: Yr1 $73,000 / Yr2 $75,000 / Yr3 $77,000 (stipend + expense allowance); $229,500 total. Plus $1,500/yr research allowance and $1,500/yr per dependent child.
deadlineInfo: Annual. Applications due June 15, 2026 (5:00pm EST).
eligibility: By the deadline: PhD received within the past 2 years OR MD within the past 4 years, and no more than 1 year in the postdoc lab (early postdoctoral training only). U.S. citizens may work in the U.S., Canada, or abroad; foreign citizens must work in U.S. labs.
description: An early-postdoctoral fellowship supporting all basic biomedical sciences, aimed at scientists at the very start of their postdoctoral training.
relevanceToUser: All basic biomedical science is eligible, so computational/quantitative blood-cancer research qualifies. Best fit for a very early postdoc (within ~1 year of starting), and open to foreign citizens working in U.S. labs.

name: Rally Foundation Research Grants (Postdoctoral/Clinical Fellow, Independent Investigator, Consortium, Outside the Box)
funder: Rally Foundation for Childhood Cancer Research
awardAmount: Postdoctoral/Clinical Research Fellow Grant: up to $50,000/yr for 1–2 yrs. Independent Investigator Grant: up to $50,000/yr for 1–2 yrs. Consortium Grant: up to $100,000/yr for 1–2 yrs. Outside the Box Grant: $50,000 for 1 yr.
deadlineInfo: Annual (2027 cycle): LOI opens Aug 3, 2026; LOI closes Sept 14, 2026 (3:30pm EDT); full application closes Jan 8, 2027 (3:30pm EST); grant term begins July 1, 2027. Submitted via ProposalCentral.
eligibility: Applicants hold MD, DO, PharmD, and/or PhD at ranks from postdoctoral fellow through Professor. Research must target childhood/pediatric cancer; the Foundation explicitly encourages under-studied cancers and data-utilization projects.
description: A pediatric-cancer research funder offering a ladder of mechanisms from postdoc fellow through established investigator, including a data-utilization and 'Outside the Box' high-risk track.
relevanceToUser: Directly relevant if the user works on pediatric hematologic malignancies (pediatric ALL/AML, lymphoma, MDS). The stated priority on data-utilization and innovative approaches makes AI/ML computational projects a natural fit; spans PhD-postdoc through faculty career stages.

name: Damon Runyon Quantitative Biology Fellowship
awardAmount: Stipend Yr1 $76,000 / Yr2 $78,000 / Yr3 $80,000, plus $2,000/yr expense allowance; ~$240,000 total.
deadlineInfo: Annual. Next deadline Dec 1, 2026 (4:00pm ET) via ProposalCentral.
eligibility: PhD, MD, MD/PhD, DDS, DVM, or DO conferred within 5 years of the deadline; no more than 24 months in the mentor's lab at deadline. Requires two mentors — one computational ('dry lab') and one cancer biologist ('wet lab'). Targets scientists trained in math, CS, physics, or engineering entering cancer research.
description: A postdoctoral fellowship purpose-built to bring quantitative/computational scientists into cancer research under joint computational + cancer-biology mentorship.
relevanceToUser: The single best-matched program for this user's profile: explicitly designed for math/CS/ML-trained postdocs applying computational methods (machine learning, modeling) to cancer, with blood-cancer projects fully eligible. Note: may overlap with another cluster's coverage, but included for completeness given its exact fit.

name: Doris Duke Clinical Scientist Development Award (CSDA)
awardAmount: $150,000/yr direct costs + $15,000/yr (10%) indirect = up to ~$495,000 over 3 years (per recent cycles).
deadlineInfo: Periodic/annual; verify current cycle on dorisduke.org (amounts confirmed from recent cycles, current-cycle dates not verified — confidence reduced accordingly).
eligibility: Early-career physician-scientists (MD, DO, or foreign equivalent) who have completed all clinical training and hold an Assistant Professor appointment at a U.S. doctoral-degree-granting 501(c)(3) institution, with an active U.S. medical license.
description: A mentored career-development award helping junior physician-scientist faculty transition to independent clinical research careers.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user is (or collaborates with) an MD hematologist-oncologist doing patient-oriented blood-cancer research; computational/clinical-data projects fit within its clinical-research scope. Less applicable to a pure-methods PhD researcher.

name: Doris Duke Physician Scientist Fellowship
awardAmount: $100,000/yr direct costs + $10,000/yr (10%) indirect = $220,000 over 2 years.
deadlineInfo: Annual; verify current cycle on dorisduke.org (amounts from published program details; current-cycle dates not verified).
eligibility: MD, MD/PhD (or foreign equivalent), or DO who are in — or have recently completed — clinical subspecialty fellowship training at the time of application. Residents in non-subspecialty programs, faculty, and assistant professors are NOT eligible.
description: A fellowship giving subspecialty clinical fellows additional protected research years to develop as clinician-investigators.
relevanceToUser: Well suited to a hematology-oncology subspecialty fellow doing blood-cancer research who wants protected time; computational/translational blood-cancer projects fit. Aimed at physician-scientist trainees rather than PhD-only researchers.

name: W.M. Keck Foundation Research Program — Medical Research (and Science & Engineering)
awardAmount: Typically $1M–$5M; most awards $2M or less (recent awards ~$1–1.5M).
deadlineInfo: Annual. Phase I concept paper due Nov 3 (4:30pm PT). Two-phase process (1-page concept paper, then invited full proposal). Institution may submit only one Phase I per program area per cycle.
eligibility: Applications must be submitted by the institution's designated Keck liaison (a limited/institutional submission), not by individuals. U.S. nonprofit institutions with 3+ years of audited financials. Funds high-risk, early-stage, paradigm-challenging fundamental research; does NOT fund disease-specific, clinical, applied, translational, or drug-development work.
description: A large-grant program for transformative, high-risk fundamental science and novel methods/instrumentation, routed through the applicant's institution rather than open individual application.
relevanceToUser: A fit only if the user frames a genuinely novel AI/ML method, algorithm, or measurement platform as fundamental science (not a disease-specific blood-cancer study, which Keck explicitly won't fund). Must go through your institution's designated Keck liaison. High reward but narrow, non-disease-specific scope.

name: TheoryLab Collaborative (TLC) Grant
awardAmount: $61,200 total for a 1-year pilot (supports a two-person cross-institution collaboration, including travel for in-person meetings).
deadlineInfo: Pilot mechanism with intermittent cycles; see the ACS grant page for current availability (dates not verified for 2026).
eligibility: Two scientists from the American Cancer Society research ecosystem (current/former ACS grantees, mentors, peer reviewers, advisers, or ACS intramural scientists) at different institutions who connect via the TheoryLab online community. Requires a pre-existing ACS relationship.
description: The ACS–Emerson Collective partnership's small pilot mechanism seeding new transdisciplinary, high-risk cancer research collaborations formed through ACS's TheoryLab platform.
relevanceToUser: This is the concrete way Emerson Collective's cancer funding reaches investigators. Good for a computational-oncology collaboration (e.g., an ML researcher pairing with a cancer biologist), but gated behind prior ACS-ecosystem membership, so not a cold-apply option.

name: Melanoma Research Alliance (MRA) Grant Program (Young Investigator, Established Investigator, Pilot, Team Science, Dermatology CDA)
awardAmount: Young Investigator Award: ~$85,000/yr × 3 yrs (~$255,000). Established Investigator Award: ~$125,000/yr × 3 yrs (~$375,000). Pilot Awards (smaller, ~1 yr proof-of-concept), Team Science Awards (larger multi-institution), and Dermatology Career Development Awards also offered. (Amounts from secondary sources; MRA's mechanism page did not list dollar figures.)
deadlineInfo: Annual RFP. Recent 2025–2026 cycle: Young Investigator eligibility checklist/biosketch ~Sept 30, 2025; full proposals ~Nov 6, 2025; awards announced ~April 2026. Verify next cycle on the MRA RFP page.
eligibility: Young Investigator: within 5 years of first academic faculty appointment, senior-investigator mentor required. Established Investigator: past first 5 years with a productivity record. Research must focus on melanoma.
description: A disease-focused foundation with a full ladder of mechanisms (young investigator through team science) that actively funds computational, immunologic, and data-driven melanoma research.
relevanceToUser: Included as a computational-cancer funding model rather than a direct blood-cancer fit — MRA funds only melanoma, so it is relevant to this user only if they apply computational/AI methods to melanoma, or as a template for how a single-disease foundation structures early-career and team-science awards.

name: Emerson Collective Fellowship
awardAmount: varies / see site (not a structured research grant)
deadlineInfo: By invitation only; no open application cycle.
eligibility: Selected through an invitation-only process based on an established track record of excellence and deep engagement in areas aligned with Emerson Collective's mission. Not a dedicated cancer-research fellowship.
description: Emerson Collective's general, invitation-only fellowship for leaders across its mission areas; its cancer-research dollars flow mainly through partnerships (e.g., the ACS TheoryLab Collaborative grant) rather than this fellowship.
relevanceToUser: Low direct relevance: there is no open cancer-specific application path here. For Emerson-linked cancer funding, the ACS TheoryLab Collaborative (TLC) Grant is the actionable mechanism. Listed for completeness so the user knows it is not a cold-apply cancer grant.

name: MMRF Research Scholars Program (Scholars Award)
awardAmount: Up to $400,000 total ($100,000/year for 4 years); plus travel reimbursement (~$1,000 for ASH, and IMS/IMW).
deadlineInfo: 2026 cycle full applications due June 30, 2026 (RFA released April 2026); annual cycle.
eligibility: Hold PhD, MD, or equivalent (or planning to defend PhD within 12 months of application); may not hold a title higher than assistant professor (or equivalent) at a U.S. institution at time of award. Clinical, translational, or basic project in multiple myeloma / precursor conditions. Black/African American applicants strongly encouraged.
description: The MMRF's flagship early-career independent-investigator award funding innovative basic, translational, or clinical myeloma research.
relevanceToUser: Directly funds computational/translational myeloma research at the assistant-professor stage; an AI/ML project (e.g., predicting progression from MGUS/smoldering myeloma, multi-omic modeling) fits the basic/translational scope, and MMRF's CoMMpass multi-omic dataset is ML-ready fuel for such work.

name: MMRF Research Fellowship Program
awardAmount: Up to $75,000 (1 year) — verify current figure on the RFA.
deadlineInfo: 2026 cycle applications due ~July 15, 2026; annual. Verify on official RFA.
eligibility: Postdoctoral researchers, clinical fellows, or junior faculty working under a qualified myeloma research mentor.
description: A one-year fellowship supporting trainees pursuing mentored multiple myeloma research.
relevanceToUser: Entry-level mechanism for a postdoc or advanced PhD student to fund a mentored computational myeloma project; lower barrier than the Scholars Award.

name: LRF Postdoctoral Fellowship Grant
awardAmount: $195,000 total over 3 years.
deadlineInfo: 2027 cycle full applications due Sept 3, 2026, 5pm EST (LOI/early-career deadline earlier). Annual cycle.
eligibility: PhD fellows with <5 years of postdoctoral work, or MD fellows with 2–5 years of fellowship, by the cycle start date. Non-profit/academic institution in U.S. or Canada; U.S./Canadian citizen or valid work visa. Reserved awards for follicular and mantle cell lymphoma projects and underrepresented applicants.
description: Supports postdoctoral fellows conducting basic, translational, or clinical lymphoma research.
relevanceToUser: Fits a computational/ML postdoc studying lymphoma (e.g., digital pathology, genomic classifiers, outcome prediction); methodology-agnostic and open to PhD-trained (non-MD) postdocs.

name: LRF Clinical Investigator Career Development Award (CDA)
awardAmount: $225,000 total over 3 years ($70,000/yr salary + $5,000/yr incidental = $75,000/yr).
deadlineInfo: 2027 cycle full applications due Sept 3, 2026, 5pm EST. Annual cycle.
eligibility: Licensed clinical physicians in ACGME-accredited specialties; advanced fellows or junior faculty transitioning to independent clinical research. U.S./Canadian citizen or valid work visa; non-profit institution. At least one award reserved for underrepresented populations/applicants.
description: Career-development award helping physician-scientists become independent clinical lymphoma researchers.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user collaborates with (or is) a physician-scientist; supports clinically-oriented lymphoma research that can incorporate AI/ML tools and diagnostics.

name: LRF Lymphoma Scientific Research Mentoring Program (LSRMP)
awardAmount: Research grant (amount varies by track) plus a $10,000 professional-development supplement for Scholars; see RFP.
deadlineInfo: 2027 cycle applications due Sept 3, 2026, 5pm EST. Annual cycle.
eligibility: Clinical/postdoctoral fellows and junior faculty (track-dependent); U.S./Canadian citizen or valid work visa. Named scholar awards for follicular lymphoma, CLL/SLL, and AYA research.
description: A combined research grant and structured mentoring program that pairs early-career lymphoma investigators with senior mentors.
relevanceToUser: Adds mentorship and a professional-development stipend on top of research funding — useful for a computational researcher building domain credibility in lymphoma; named-scholar tracks (CLL/SLL, follicular) align with hematologic-malignancy focus.

name: AAMDSIF Research Grants Program
awardAmount: $80,000 total ($40,000/year for 2 years).
deadlineInfo: 2026 cycle: portal opens April 10, 2026; LOI due June 30, 2026; full application due Oct 31, 2026; funding begins Jan 1, 2027. Annual (LOI required).
eligibility: New Investigators only — postdoctoral fellows, instructors, or those who have been assistant professors for fewer than 5 years. Projects on aplastic anemia, MDS, or PNH; one award earmarked for aplastic anemia. No citizenship restriction specified.
description: Seed grants for new investigators studying bone marrow failure diseases (aplastic anemia, MDS, PNH).
relevanceToUser: One of the few funders squarely focused on MDS and bone-marrow-failure syndromes — a strong fit for AI/ML on MDS genomics, clonal-hematopoiesis modeling, or bone-marrow image/transcriptomic analysis. Two-year seed size suits a focused computational pilot.

name: Gabrielle's Angel Foundation Medical Research Award
funder: Gabrielle's Angel Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $225,000 total over 3 years.
deadlineInfo: Invitation-only RFP released each spring; applications due in August; decisions in the first half of the following year. Annual.
eligibility: Invitation-only — RFP sent only to NCI-designated institutions, which nominate eligible investigators. Typically Assistant Professors (Associate Professors eligible only for integrative-medicine grants). Focus on leukemia, lymphoma, and related blood cancers translatable to clinical trials within 3–5 years. International proposals not accepted.
description: Early-career blood-cancer research awards emphasizing translational leukemia/lymphoma work with near-term clinical potential.
relevanceToUser: Directly targets leukemia/lymphoma/blood-cancer research at the assistant-professor stage; your institution is not NCI-designated, so the user would need to route through a nominating NCI-designated cancer center collaborator (e.g., Dana-Farber) — worth noting the invitation-only constraint.

name: ALSF 'A' Award
awardAmount: Up to $200,000 total ($100,000/year for 2 years); no indirect costs (direct research costs only).
deadlineInfo: 2026 cycle full application due April 30, 2026; decisions announced June 26, 2026. Annual. (Note: award size was historically larger/$600K over 4 yrs in prior years — verify current RFA.)
eligibility: MD, MD/PhD, or PhD within the first 5 years of their first independent faculty appointment (assistant professor or equivalent) at a non-profit U.S. or Canadian institution; historically also 'within 10 years of terminal degree.' Project must focus on childhood cancer. Cannot hold another concurrent 'A' Award.
description: ALSF's early-independent-investigator award to help launch a pediatric-oncology research career.
relevanceToUser: Ideal for a newly-appointed faculty member doing AI/ML on pediatric leukemia (e.g., ALL/AML subtyping, MRD prediction). ALSF is unusually receptive to computational and data-science projects (see its Childhood Cancer Data Lab).

name: ALSF Young Investigator Grant
awardAmount: Up to $180,000 total (~$60,000/year for up to 3 years) — verify exact figure on the 2026 guidelines.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle (recent full-application deadlines in spring; LOI earlier). Verify current dates via ALSF grants calendar.
eligibility: Early-career investigators (fellows/instructors/early junior faculty) still under mentorship, conducting childhood-cancer research at a non-profit institution. Verify years-from-degree limits on current guidelines.
description: Mentored early-career grant cultivating the next generation of pediatric-oncology scientists.
relevanceToUser: A good mentored-stage fit for a computational postdoc entering pediatric-leukemia research; ALSF's data-science emphasis makes ML methods projects competitive.

name: ALSF Innovation Grant
awardAmount: $250,000 total over 2 years.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle (LOI typically fall/winter, full application winter/spring). Verify via ALSF grants calendar.
eligibility: Independent/established investigators (typically faculty with a track record) pursuing high-risk, high-reward novel approaches in pediatric oncology at a non-profit institution.
description: Seed funding for bold, unconventional pediatric-cancer research ideas that lack preliminary data.
relevanceToUser: The high-risk/high-reward framing is well-suited to novel AI/ML methods for pediatric leukemia where preliminary data is thin; ALSF explicitly welcomes computational innovation.

name: ALSF Reach Grant
awardAmount: See site (recent Reach Grants ~$250,000 over 2 years; verify current RFA).
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle; recent full-application deadline was April 10, 2025. Verify current dates.
eligibility: Investigators with promising preclinical, hypothesis-driven pediatric-cancer projects poised to move toward a Phase I/II trial within 2–3 years of project end. Non-profit institution.
description: Translational grant to bridge preclinical pediatric-cancer findings into early-phase clinical trials.
relevanceToUser: Fits computational work that de-risks a therapeutic hypothesis (e.g., ML-driven target/biomarker prioritization) with a clear translational path in pediatric leukemia.

name: ALSF Pediatric Oncology Student Training (POST) Grant
awardAmount: Stipend-level award (varies; see current guidelines).
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle; verify dates on ALSF grants calendar.
eligibility: Students (including graduate students) undertaking a mentored pediatric-oncology research project at a non-profit institution.
description: Supports students conducting mentored childhood-cancer research to seed future pediatric-oncology careers.
relevanceToUser: A route for a computational PhD student to get seed funding for a pediatric-leukemia data/ML project and enter the field.

name: ALSF Childhood Cancer Data Lab (CCDL) — data-science training & Catalyst/OpenScPCA opportunities
awardAmount: Varies (free training/workshops; funded single-cell/data projects such as ScPCA and OpenScPCA; CCDL postdoctoral training grants). See site.
deadlineInfo: Rolling / periodic (workshops, e.g., virtual single-cell RNA-seq training June 2026; project calls announced periodically). See CCDL site.
eligibility: Open to childhood-cancer researchers across career stages; some project funding tied to contributing data to the Single-cell Pediatric Cancer Atlas (ScPCA)/OpenScPCA.
description: ALSF's in-house data-science arm offering free computational training, open pediatric-cancer datasets (ScPCA/OpenScPCA), and associated funding for data-generating projects.
relevanceToUser: The single most methods-aligned opportunity in this cluster: an AI/ML researcher can access ML-ready pediatric-cancer single-cell datasets, collaborate on OpenScPCA, and tap CCDL training/funding — directly matching the user's data-science + computational-oncology profile.

name: St. Baldrick's Scholar (Career Development) Award
awardAmount: Up to $110,000/year for years 1–3, and up to $115,000/year for years 4–5 (conditional).
duration: 3 years, with option for 2 additional years based on progress
deadlineInfo: 2026 cycle; institutional limited-submission with LOI/pre-application in fall 2025 and full application thereafter. Annual. Verify exact dates.
eligibility: Highly qualified investigators still early in their pediatric-cancer research careers, establishing independent research; non-profit institution. Often a limited-submission (institution-nominated) program.
description: Multi-year career-development award to launch independent pediatric-cancer researchers.
relevanceToUser: Substantial multi-year support for a new PI doing AI/ML on pediatric leukemia; St. Baldrick's funds laboratory, clinical, AND epidemiological/computational studies.

name: St. Baldrick's Fellowship Award
awardAmount: Up to $97,500/year for 2 years ($195,000 total), with a potential additional year based on need/accomplishment.
deadlineInfo: 2026 cycle; institutional limited-submission with pre-application in spring 2025 and full application later. Annual. Verify dates.
eligibility: Trainees typically in their 2nd or 3rd year of pediatric-oncology fellowship by July 1 of the award year; non-profit institution.
description: Training award supporting pediatric-oncology fellows conducting childhood-cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Best suited to a physician-fellow collaborator; less applicable to a pure-computational PhD/postdoc since it targets clinical pediatric-oncology fellows.

name: St. Baldrick's Research Grant
awardAmount: Up to $100,000/year for 2 years (up to $200,000 total).
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle; verify LOI/application dates on St. Baldrick's site.
eligibility: Independent investigators proposing hypothesis-driven laboratory, clinical, OR epidemiological studies advancing childhood-cancer understanding and treatment; non-profit institution.
description: Project grant for hypothesis-driven childhood-cancer research across lab, clinical, and epidemiological domains.
relevanceToUser: The explicit inclusion of epidemiological/quantitative studies makes this a clean fit for AI/ML and computational-epidemiology projects in pediatric leukemia.

name: St. Baldrick's Robert J. Arceci Innovation Award
awardAmount: See site (multi-year, larger innovation award; verify amount on official RFA).
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle; verify dates on St. Baldrick's site.
eligibility: Early- to mid-career scientists demonstrating creativity, collaboration, and commitment to childhood-cancer research; non-profit institution.
description: Named innovation award honoring Dr. Robert Arceci, rewarding creative and collaborative pediatric-cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Innovation framing rewards novel, cross-disciplinary methods — a fit for an AI/ML researcher bringing new computational approaches to pediatric leukemia.

name: AACR-St. Baldrick's Foundation Pediatric Cancer Research Grant
funder: St. Baldrick's Foundation / American Association for Cancer Research (AACR)
awardAmount: See site (jointly funded career-development grant; verify current amount/duration on AACR page).
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle via AACR; verify dates on the AACR funded-research page.
eligibility: Early-career pediatric-cancer researchers (career-development level); specific degree/years-from-degree criteria on the AACR RFA. Non-profit institution.
description: A co-funded AACR/St. Baldrick's career-development grant for pediatric-cancer investigators.
relevanceToUser: Another pediatric-leukemia career-development route; administered through AACR, whose review is receptive to computational/quantitative cancer science.

name: Bone Marrow Failure Research Program (BMFRP) — Idea Development Award (IDA)
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP (Congressionally Directed Medical Research Programs), Defense Health Program
awardAmount: FY24: up to $530,000 direct costs (verified, official). FY26 listed as total-cost cap ~$800,000 (per program summaries; CDMRP switched to total-cost caps). Confirm current-year figure on eBRAP announcement.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. FY26 pre-application (Grants.gov/eBRAP) ~Aug and Oct; full application ~Nov. FY24 example: pre-app July 17, 2024. Verify current dates on the FY26 announcement.
eligibility: Open to two PI tracks: Established Investigators (independent, Associate Professor or above, or Assistant Prof >10 yrs) and Early-Career Investigators (independent, Assistant Professor level, <10 years from that appointment — this is the 'New Investigator' path). U.S. citizenship NOT required; foreign/domestic non-profit, for-profit, and academic organizations eligible.
description: Supports innovative, high-impact ideas (with or without preliminary data) to understand and cure inherited and acquired bone marrow failure diseases — aplastic anemia, MDS, Fanconi anemia, and related disorders. The ECI track lowers the bar for newly independent faculty.
relevanceToUser: Directly targets the user's MDS/bone marrow failure interest. Preliminary data is not required, so a novel AI/ML or computational-biology approach to BMF disease mechanisms, risk prediction, or clonal-evolution modeling fits well. The Early-Career Investigator track suits a new your institution faculty member.

name: Bone Marrow Failure Research Program (BMFRP) — Investigator-Initiated Research Award (IIRA)
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP, Defense Health Program
awardAmount: Total-cost cap ~$1,250,000 (FY26, per program summaries). Historical FY23 direct-cost caps were ~$675,000 (Funding Level 1) / ~$850,000 (Funding Level 2). Verify current figure on eBRAP.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle alongside the IDA (FY26 pre-app ~Aug/Oct, full app ~Nov). Verify on the FY26 announcement.
eligibility: Independent investigators at all career levels; typically requires stronger preliminary data than the IDA. Partnering PI option available. No citizenship restriction.
description: Funds more mature, hypothesis-driven bone marrow failure research building on prior findings and preliminary data, with potential to significantly advance the field or patient care.
relevanceToUser: Good fit once the user has generated preliminary computational results in MDS/BMF; supports larger, better-powered projects and a partnering-PI structure ideal for a stats/ML lab collaborating with a wet-lab or clinical hematology group.

name: Bone Marrow Failure Research Program (BMFRP) — Resource Development Award (RDA)
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP, Defense Health Program
awardAmount: Total-cost cap ~$1,250,000 (FY26, per program summaries). Verify on the eBRAP announcement.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle with the other BMFRP mechanisms (FY26 pre-app ~Aug/Oct). Verify current dates on the FY26 announcement.
eligibility: Independent investigators at all career levels. No citizenship restriction.
description: Supports creation of shared resources, tools, datasets, models, or infrastructure that benefit the broader bone marrow failure research community (one of the program's three required focus areas is developing durable resources).
relevanceToUser: Strong fit for a data-science/ML researcher: explicitly funds building datasets, computational tools, and shared infrastructure — e.g., a curated multi-omic BMF/MDS dataset, a benchmark, or an ML model/pipeline released as a community resource. This is one of the few CDMRP mechanisms tailored to method/resource development rather than a single hypothesis.

name: Peer Reviewed Cancer Research Program (PRCRP) — Idea Award (incl. new Early Career Investigator Option)
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP, Defense Health Program
awardAmount: Total-cost cap $600,000 per award (FY26, verified). Early Career Investigator Option same $600,000 cap (~15 ECI awards planned).
deadlineInfo: FY26: pre-application (LOI) due June 26, 2026; full application due October 5, 2026 (verified). Annual cycle; topic areas set yearly by Congress.
eligibility: Two options. Established Investigator: no career-stage limit. Early Career Investigator Option (new for FY26): within 7 years of last postdoctoral research position (PhD) or clinical fellowship (MD), AND received less than $150,000/yr direct-cost non-mentored research funding. Application must address a required FY26 PRCRP topic area. No citizenship restriction.
description: Funds innovative, untested, high-risk/high-reward cancer research; preliminary data not required. The Blood Cancers portfolio topic areas are Blood (includes leukemia), Lymphoma, and Myeloma. Congressionally excluded cancers are breast, prostate, ovarian, and melanoma.
relevanceToUser: Best general-purpose DoD entry point for the user's blood-cancer work: leukemia, lymphoma, and myeloma are explicit topic areas, no preliminary data is required (ideal for a novel AI/ML concept), and the new Early Career Investigator Option is well-matched to a recent postdoc or new faculty member.

name: Peer Reviewed Cancer Research Program (PRCRP) — Career Development Award – Scholar Option (CDA-SO)
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP, Defense Health Program
awardAmount: Total-cost cap $1.1M per award (FY25, verified; ~9 awards, ~$9.9M total).
deadlineInfo: FY25: pre-application due June 20, 2025; full application due July 3, 2025. Offered in some fiscal years (not every year); confirm whether it recurs in the current PRCRP solicitation.
eligibility: PI (the 'Scholar') must be an independent early-career investigator within 7 years of completing their terminal degree (postdoctoral fellows are NOT eligible), holding independent lab space, and committing >=25% effort for the first 2 years. Must designate a Career Guide (mentor) at Associate Professor level or above with a strong cancer-research funding/publication and mentorship record. Research must address a PRCRP topic area (Blood, Lymphoma, Myeloma qualify).
description: A mentored career-development mechanism for newly independent early-career cancer investigators, providing up to 4 years and $1.1M to establish an independent research program under an experienced Career Guide.
relevanceToUser: The strongest career-development fit for a newly independent early-career faculty member in computational oncology/blood cancers. A statistics/ML PI can pair with a senior hematologic-oncology Career Guide (they need not be at the same institution). Note: postdocs are not eligible — use it once independent.

name: Peer Reviewed Cancer Research Program (PRCRP) — Impact Award
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP, Defense Health Program
awardAmount: See site (larger, translational-scale award; amount not verified this pass).
deadlineInfo: FY26: pre-application due June 26, 2026; full application due October 5, 2026 (shared PRCRP cycle). Verify cap/eligibility on the announcement.
eligibility: Independent investigators; intended for higher-impact/translational cancer projects that must address a required FY26 PRCRP topic area. Verify specific eligibility on the official announcement.
description: One of the three FY26 PRCRP mechanisms (with the Idea Award and Clinical Trial Award). Supports higher-impact, more developed cancer research within the annual PRCRP topic areas, which include Blood, Lymphoma, and Myeloma.
relevanceToUser: A step-up mechanism for blood-cancer projects with strong preliminary evidence and clear translational impact; relevant once the user's computational findings are ready to drive a larger, more mature study. (The PRCRP Clinical Trial Award also exists but requires running a trial, so it is a weaker fit for a methods lab.)

name: Rare Cancers Research Program (RCRP) — Idea Development, Concept, and Resource & Community Development Awards
funder: U.S. Department of Defense — CDMRP, Defense Health Program
awardAmount: See site (per-mechanism caps not verified this pass).
deadlineInfo: FY26 (release June 17, 2026): Idea Development Award pre-app Aug 19, app Nov 18, 2026; Concept Award LOI Sept 16, app Sept 30, 2026; Resource & Community Development Award pre-app Aug 19, app Nov 18, 2026.
eligibility: Focuses on rare cancers (incidence below a defined threshold) not covered by other CDMRP disease-specific programs. Rare hematologic malignancies/subtypes (e.g., rare leukemias, lymphoma subtypes, myeloproliferative neoplasms) can qualify. Verify topic eligibility and career-stage rules on the official announcement.
description: Supports research on rare cancers, including a Concept Award (small, exploratory), an Idea Development Award (larger hypothesis-driven), and a Resource & Community Development Award (datasets/tools/community infrastructure).
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user's work targets rare blood-cancer subtypes underserved by other programs. The Concept Award is a low-barrier way to pilot a novel ML idea, and the Resource & Community Development Award suits building shared computational datasets/tools for rare hematologic cancers. Confirm the specific subtype qualifies as 'rare' under the current RCRP definition.

name: NIH Research Project Grant (Parent R01)
funder: NIH — National Cancer Institute (NCI), NHLBI, NIDDK and other ICs
awardAmount: No fixed cap; modular budgets up to $250,000/yr direct costs (in $25K modules). Requests of $500,000/yr direct or more require prior IC approval. Budgets 'must reflect actual needs.'
deadlineInfo: Standing NOFO (open ~3 yrs). Standard new-application due dates Feb 5 / Jun 5 / Oct 5 annually (AIDS: Jan 7 / May 7 / Sep 7).
eligibility: Domestic and foreign institutions; independent investigators in an eligible faculty/PI appointment. No PI citizenship requirement. CT Not Allowed = PA-25-301; CT Required = PA-25-305.
description: The flagship investigator-initiated research project grant funding a discrete, hypothesis-driven project. NCI, NHLBI, and NIDDK all participate, so the same mechanism serves cancer, blood-cancer, and blood-disease research.
relevanceToUser: The primary vehicle for an independent your institution faculty member to fund an AI/ML-driven computational oncology or hematologic-malignancy research program. Route to NCI for leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS work; to NHLBI/NIDDK for non-malignant blood/marrow-failure work.

name: NIH Exploratory/Developmental Research Grant (Parent R21)
funder: NIH — NCI, NHLBI, NIDDK and other ICs
awardAmount: Up to $275,000 total direct costs over the 2-year period, with no more than $200,000 in any single year.
deadlineInfo: Standing NOFO. Standard new-application due dates Feb 16 / Jun 16 / Oct 16 annually.
eligibility: Same institutional eligibility as R01; suited to novel/high-risk pilot work with no strict preliminary-data requirement. CT Not Allowed = PA-25-304; CT Required = PA-25-306.
description: Exploratory/developmental grant for early-stage, higher-risk projects — well suited to proof-of-concept method development where preliminary data are limited.
relevanceToUser: Good fit for testing a new AI/ML method on cancer or blood-cancer data before committing to a full R01. Preliminary data are not required, lowering the barrier for a novel computational approach.

name: NCI Method to Extend Research in Time (MERIT) Award (R37)
awardAmount: Same budget as the underlying R01; provides up to 7 years of support (initial award up to 5 years plus a possible 2-year extension after NCI review).
deadlineInfo: No separate application. Qualifying ESI R01 applications scoring within the NCI ESI payline are automatically considered for conversion; follow R01 standard due dates.
eligibility: Early Stage Investigators (ESI) — within 10 years of terminal degree/clinical training and no prior substantial NIH independent research award — whose R01 falls within the NCI payline. All project leaders must retain ESI status at award.
description: An NCI honorific extension of the R01 that gives high-scoring early-stage investigators longer, more flexible funding. Cannot be applied for directly — it is a conversion of a competitive ESI R01.
relevanceToUser: If a first R01 on AI-for-oncology scores within the NCI ESI payline, it can convert to up to 7 years of stable funding — valuable for a new your institution faculty member building a computational cancer program.

name: Informatics Technology for Cancer Research (ITCR) Program (U01 / U24)
funder: NCI Center for Strategic Scientific Initiatives (CSSI)
awardAmount: Early-Stage Development (RFA-CA-27-019, U01): up to $300,000 direct costs/yr for up to 3 yrs. Advanced Development (RFA-CA-27-020, U24) and Sustained Support (RFA-CA-27-021, U24): up to $600,000 direct costs/yr for up to 5 yrs.
duration: 3 years (U01 early-stage) or up to 5 years (U24 advanced/sustained)
deadlineInfo: Set-receipt-date RFAs reissued on a recurring cycle; check the ITCR funding page and the specific RFA for the current due date.
eligibility: Independent investigators at eligible institutions developing informatics tools/software across the cancer research continuum. Three-tier pipeline from prototype (U01) to enhancement/dissemination and long-term sustainment (U24).
description: NCI's dedicated program for building and sustaining cancer informatics software and computational tools, spanning early prototype development through maintenance of widely adopted resources.
relevanceToUser: The single most on-target NCI mechanism for a statistics/ML lab building reusable AI/computational tools for cancer (including hematologic malignancy) — funds algorithm/software development, not just a single hypothesis-driven study.

name: NCI Research Specialist Award (R50)
awardAmount: Salary support (up to the NIH salary cap) plus limited travel funds; typically ~5 years and renewable. Requires a minimum 6 person-months (50%) effort on NCI-funded research.
deadlineInfo: Recurring PAR reissued periodically (recent versions e.g. laboratory/core-based PAR-23-242 and clinician-scientist PAR-24-274 have expired). Check the NCI R50 page for the current open NOFO and due dates.
eligibility: Advanced-degree scientists (explicitly including data scientists) who have contributed ≥2 years to a cancer research program, devote ≥50% time to NCI-funded research, do NOT wish to be independent PIs, and have not sought independent-investigator funding in the last ~5 years.
description: Provides stable, independent salary support for exceptional staff/computational scientists so they are not solely dependent on others' grants — created in part to retain data scientists in cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Ideal for a computational/AI scientist who wants a durable research-staff career analyzing cancer data within a lab or core rather than running their own R01-funded program.

name: NCI Predoctoral to Postdoctoral Fellow Transition Award (F99/K00)
awardAmount: F99 phase (up to 2 yrs): NRSA predoctoral stipend, tuition/fees, institutional allowance. K00 phase (up to 3 yrs): up to $80,000/yr salary + fringe, plus ~$30,000/yr research/career-development support.
duration: Up to 5 years total (F99 up to 2 + K00 up to 3)
deadlineInfo: Annual receipt cycle; institutions may nominate a limited number. See NOFO PA-27-037 for the current due date.
eligibility: Advanced PhD candidates (typically 1–2 years from completion) who are U.S. citizens, nationals, or permanent residents by award; bridges dissertation to a mentored postdoc in cancer research.
description: A single award that spans the end of the PhD and the start of a postdoc, giving outstanding cancer-research trainees continuous, portable support across the transition.
relevanceToUser: For a late-stage PhD student in an ML lab whose dissertation has a cancer/blood-cancer focus, this de-risks the move into a computational cancer postdoc with up to 5 years of guaranteed funding.

name: NCI Pathway to Independence Award, including Early K99/R00
awardAmount: K99 (mentored, 1–2 yrs): up to $100,000/yr salary + fringe plus up to $30,000/yr research support. R00 (independent, 1–3 yrs): up to $249,000/yr total costs.
duration: Up to 5 years (K99 up to 2 + R00 up to 3)
deadlineInfo: NCI's Early K99/R00 uses PAR-23-286 (CT Not Allowed), PAR-23-287 (CT Required), PAR-23-288 (BESH). Standard K-series due dates (~Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12); institutional nomination required. Note NCI left the NIH parent K99/R00 (PA-24-193/194) in Jan 2025.
eligibility: Postdoctoral fellows; NCI's Early K99 targets those with ≤2 years of postdoctoral experience at the due date. No citizenship requirement. One of four named tracks is explicitly Cancer Data Science (AI/ML, informatics, genomics).
description: The premier postdoc-to-faculty transition grant: mentored K99 phase followed by an independent R00 activated when the awardee starts a faculty position, with a dedicated Cancer Data Science track.
relevanceToUser: Directly aimed at a computational/AI postdoc aiming for a faculty job — the Cancer Data Science track is written for AI/ML/informatics researchers, and the R00 provides start-up funding at the new institution.

name: NCI Ruth L. Kirschstein NRSA Individual Predoctoral Fellowship (F31)
awardAmount: 2026 predoctoral stipend $29,364/yr; NCI also covers 60% of tuition/fees (capped at $16,000/yr) and up to $4,750/yr for insurance, supplies, and travel.
deadlineInfo: Three cycles per year: Apr 8 / Aug 8 / Dec 8. NOFO PA-25-422 (parent F31).
eligibility: PhD candidates at the dissertation-research stage with a clear cancer focus; U.S. citizen or permanent resident at award (not required at application). NCI recognizes focus categories including Cancer Data Science.
description: Individual predoctoral fellowship providing stipend and training support for a dissertation with a cancer focus, submitted through the NCI under the parent F31 with cancer-specific review categories.
relevanceToUser: The entry-level mechanism for a PhD student in an ML/statistics lab whose thesis applies AI to cancer or blood-cancer problems; the Cancer Data Science category makes computational projects competitive.

name: NCI Individual Predoctoral MD/PhD Fellowship (F30)
awardAmount: NRSA predoctoral stipend plus tuition/fees and institutional allowance, structured across the research and clinical phases of dual-degree training.
deadlineInfo: Three cycles per year (~Apr 8 / Aug 8 / Dec 8), submitted through parent F30 (PA-25-425 / PA-25-426).
eligibility: Students in an accredited MD/PhD (or other dual clinical-doctoral/PhD) program pursuing cancer-relevant dissertation research; U.S. citizen or permanent resident at award.
description: Dual-degree predoctoral fellowship supporting the integrated research and clinical training of future physician-scientists in cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Relevant for an MD/PhD trainee combining clinical hematology/oncology with computational/AI dissertation work — the physician-scientist entry point in the pipeline.

name: Mentored Clinical Scientist Career Development Award (Parent K08) — with K23 companion
funder: NIH — NCI, NHLBI, NIDDK and other ICs
awardAmount: Salary/fringe for ≥75% (9 mo) protected effort plus research-development funds (supplies, equipment, personnel, travel, statistical support); amounts set by each IC. Indirect costs at 8% MTDC.
deadlineInfo: Standing NOFO; standard K due dates ~Feb 12 / Jun 12 / Oct 12. K08 = PA-24-182 (CT Not Allowed) / PA-24-181 (CT Required). Patient-oriented companion K23 = PA-24-185 / PA-24-184.
eligibility: Clinicians with a doctoral degree (MD, DO, DVM, DDS, PharmD, or clinical PhD) seeking protected time to become independent researchers. K08 for lab/translational research; K23 for patient-oriented research. NCI, NHLBI, and NIDDK all participate.
description: Mentored career-development ('K') awards giving physician-scientists several years of protected time to establish an independent research program in cancer or blood disease.
relevanceToUser: For a clinician collaborator or physician-scientist embedding AI/computational methods into hematologic-malignancy or blood-disease research; NCI funds the cancer angle, NHLBI/NIDDK the non-malignant blood angle.

name: NHLBI Blood Diseases and Resources research funding
funder: NHLBI — Division of Blood Diseases and Resources (DBDR)
awardAmount: Varies by mechanism (uses parent R01/R21/R03/F/K plus NHLBI-specific NOFOs and center programs such as EHRA and the Cure Sickle Cell Initiative).
deadlineInfo: Investigator-initiated via parent-announcement standard dates; targeted NHLBI NOFOs have their own set dates — see the NHLBI Funding Opportunities and Contacts page and program officers.
eligibility: Investigators studying non-malignant blood diseases and blood resources — bone marrow failure biology, hematopoiesis, transfusion medicine, HSCT, and novel cell therapies. NHLBI generally funds non-cancer blood cell diseases (blood cancers are NCI's remit).
description: NHLBI's blood-diseases portfolio spans three program areas (Blood Epidemiology & Clinical Therapeutics; Molecular, Cellular & Systems Blood Science; Translational Blood Science & Resources) plus data-science efforts like BioData Catalyst and TOPMed.
relevanceToUser: The home for computational/AI work on the non-malignant side of hematology (e.g., bone marrow failure, hematopoiesis, HSCT outcomes) that borders the user's MDS/marrow-failure interests; contact a DBDR program officer to match a mechanism.

name: NIDDK Hematology Program (KUH) & Cooperative Centers of Excellence in Hematology
funder: NIDDK — Division of Kidney, Urologic, and Hematologic Diseases (KUH)
awardAmount: Varies — investigator-initiated grants (parent R01/R21/F/K) plus consortium/center and training awards (e.g., Cooperative Centers of Excellence in Hematology; KUH training network U2C/TL1).
deadlineInfo: Investigator-initiated via parent-announcement standard dates; center/network RFAs (e.g., recent KUH RFA-DK-27-126) have set dates. See the NIDDK KUH division page and program officers.
eligibility: Researchers in non-malignant hematology — including inherited and acquired bone marrow failure (aplastic anemia, Fanconi anemia, Diamond-Blackfan, dyskeratosis congenita), red-cell biology, and iron metabolism (adult and pediatric).
description: NIDDK's KUH division supports non-malignant hematology and runs the Cooperative Centers of Excellence in Hematology (CCEH), which provide shared cores/reagents to the marrow-failure research community.
relevanceToUser: Best NIDDK fit for computational work on inherited/acquired bone marrow failure biology; note the malignant progression (MDS/leukemia) itself falls under NCI, while NIDDK covers the non-malignant marrow-failure mechanisms and offers core resources.

name: NIH Bridge to Artificial Intelligence (Bridge2AI) — Common Fund
funder: NIH Common Fund (Office of the Director)
awardAmount: Program-level: ~$130M over 4 years in Stage 1 (large team U54/OT2 data-generation and BRIDGE Center awards). Stage 2 budget per-award TBD.
deadlineInfo: No open individual-PI NOFO as of mid-2025 (funding page: 'no funding opportunities at this time'). Stage 2 was approved by the NIH Council of Councils on Jan 29, 2026 — watch for new NOFOs (Innovation Funnels; Network for AI Health Science).
eligibility: Primarily large multidisciplinary teams generating ethically sourced, AI-ready datasets, ML best practices, tools, and standards; not a single-PI training grant.
description: NIH's cross-institute flagship for making biomedical data AI-ready and building responsible-AI methods, tools, and standards — the closest thing to a dedicated NIH AI/ML program.
relevanceToUser: Most relevant NIH-wide AI program for a methods/ML lab; teams can contribute AI/ML methodology and standards. Cancer/blood datasets could intersect. Monitor for Stage 2 NOFOs since nothing is currently open to apply to.

name: AI2050 Early Career Fellowship
funder: Schmidt Sciences (Schmidt Futures / Eric and Wendy Schmidt)
focusAreas: beneficial AI / hard problems in AI
awardAmount: Up to $300,000 over 2 years (official site); recent 2025 cohort funded 3-year projects, $18M across 28 fellows
deadlineInfo: No open application — closed nomination process; ~15-21 early-career fellows selected annually, cohorts announced ~Nov (latest Nov 2025)
eligibility: Postdoctoral and pre-tenure researchers worldwide pursuing bold work on AI2050's 'hard problems' in AI; selected via closed nomination (cannot self-apply); no citizenship restriction stated
description: Enabling fellowship for early-career researchers working on hard problems that must be solved for AI to be hugely beneficial by 2050, including building AI scientists and safe/trustworthy AI.
relevanceToUser: Explicitly funds AI-for-science and biological/medical AI: the 2025 cohort included fellows combining specialized scientific models (e.g., AlphaFold-class) with reasoning systems and AI for drug discovery — ML methods for computational oncology fit the 'AI scientist' and beneficial-AI themes. Worth cultivating nominators since there is no open application.

name: Schmidt Science Fellows
funder: Schmidt Sciences / Schmidt Science Fellows (in partnership with Rhodes Trust)
awardAmount: $110,000/year personal stipend (widely documented; not restated on official FAQ)
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2027 cohort: institutional nominations due May 15, 2026; portal opened May 19, 2026; full applications due July 13, 2026; cohort announced March 2027
eligibility: Must complete PhD (incl. defense) between May 1, 2026 and June 30, 2027 (for 2027 cohort) in natural sciences, engineering, math, or computing; must be nominated by PhD-awarding institution (MIT is a nominating partner); must pivot to a discipline different from the PhD; clinical-track MD-PhD not eligible
description: Prestigious postdoctoral fellowship funding a deliberate interdisciplinary pivot away from the PhD discipline, with placement in a new lab plus a science-leadership program.
relevanceToUser: A textbook fit for an ML/statistics PhD pivoting into cancer biology/hematologic malignancies — the required 'interdisciplinary pivot' (e.g., ML methods PhD -> experimental/computational oncology lab) is exactly what the program rewards. Only applicable near PhD completion via your institution internal nomination.

name: Sloan Research Fellowship
deadlineInfo: Annual. Nominations for 2027 fellowships open July 15, 2026; materials historically due Sept 15 (2026 cycle deadline was Sept 15, 2025)
eligibility: PhD in chemistry, CS, economics, math, neuroscience, earth system science, physics or related field; tenure-track but untenured faculty at a US/Canadian degree-granting institution with a regular teaching obligation; nominated by department head/senior researcher (max 3 nominees per department per field)
description: Early-career honorific fellowship providing flexible research funds to the most promising untenured faculty in eight STEM fields.
relevanceToUser: An your institution statistics/ML researcher would be nominated in Computer Science or Mathematics; ML methodology with applications to computational oncology is well within scope (fellows are judged on methodological/scientific promise, and health applications do not disqualify). Only relevant once in a tenure-track faculty position.

name: Packard Fellowships for Science and Engineering
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2026 cycle: institutional letters of intent due March 15, 2026; applications due April 20, 2026; internal university pre-selection happens months earlier
eligibility: Faculty in first 3 years of first tenure-track appointment (2026 cycle: appointment began no earlier than May 31, 2023) at one of 50 invited US institutions (MIT included); each president nominates up to 2 candidates; fields include CS, math, biology, engineering; clinical research and social science excluded
description: One of the largest unrestricted early-career awards in US science: 20 fellows/year receive flexible five-year funding for blue-sky research.
relevanceToUser: AI/ML methods for cancer biology qualifies under CS/biology/engineering as long as it is framed as basic (not clinical) research — computational modeling of leukemia/lymphoma biology is fine, clinical-trial work is not. Requires winning your institution's internal nomination (2 slots).

name: Targeted Grants in Mathematics and Physical Sciences (MPS)
awardAmount: varies — flexible, no recommended funding level
deadlineInfo: Rolling — LOIs accepted anytime via Simons Award Manager; LOI reviewed within ~3 months, invited full proposals ~3 more months
eligibility: PI/co-Is must hold tenured/tenure-track (or equivalent) positions at established US or foreign educational/research institutions; no citizenship restrictions; theory-focused projects (some experimental components possible). Note: the flagship Simons Investigators program has been discontinued per the foundation's site
description: Case-by-case support for high-risk theoretical mathematics, physics and computer science projects of exceptional promise, with flexible budgets and rolling submission.
relevanceToUser: Best current Simons entry point for a statistics/ML theorist: foundations-of-ML, mathematical/statistical theory, and mathematical modeling qualify; direct blood-cancer application work would need to be framed as theory-driven. (Simons Investigators is discontinued; Junior Fellows requires NYC-area institutions; Pivot Fellowship currently closed.)

name: Google PhD Fellowship
careerStages: PhD student (any stage; coursework complete before start)
awardAmount: US/Canada: $85,000/year toward tuition, fees, living expenses, travel and equipment (effective 2025)
deadlineInfo: Annual spring cycle. 2026 cycle: opened March 5, closed April 30, 2026; decisions by Aug 31, 2026 — next cycle expected ~March 2027
eligibility: Must be nominated by the university (no direct applications; US/Canada universities nominate up to 4 students); enrolled full-time in PhD for award duration; cannot hold a comparable industry award (government/non-profit funding OK); not open to prior awardees or Google employees' households
description: Recognizes outstanding PhD students in computer science and related areas with full support and a Google research mentor.
relevanceToUser: Health & Bioscience (medical data analysis) and Machine Intelligence are explicit award tracks — ML for computational oncology/blood cancers fits squarely. Requires winning your institution's internal nomination (up to 4 slots), so start with the department early in the year.

name: Google Research Scholar Program (status uncertain; see Google Academic Research Awards)
awardAmount: Historically up to $60,000 unrestricted gift
deadlineInfo: No announced 2026 open call; Aug 2025 round funded 87 recipients ($4.7M) and Google says it is 'evolving' the program — check site. The successor open call, Google Academic Research Awards, ran Jun 26-Aug 7, 2026 (2026 cycle limited to quantum computing topics)
eligibility: Professors (assistant/associate) within 7 years of PhD at degree-granting institutions worldwide; max 3 applications within the 7-year window; one proposal per PI per round. Google Academic Research Awards: professors advising students, topic-restricted per cycle
description: Google's early-career faculty funding vehicle; the classic Research Scholar open call appears to be winding down in favor of Google Academic Research Awards and Google.org ecosystem funding (including a $20M AI-and-science fund).
relevanceToUser: Historically a strong fit (health/ML topics eligible, $60K unrestricted for junior faculty), but as of mid-2026 there is no announced new Research Scholar round and the active successor call is quantum-only — monitor research.google for a health/AI cycle before planning on it.

name: Microsoft Research Fellowship (successor to the Microsoft Research PhD Fellowship)
awardAmount: $47,000 one-time unrestricted gift (US; includes travel for collaboration); other regions $17,000
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2026 cycle applications closed early December 2025 (reported deadline Dec 2, 2025); next cycle expected fall 2026 — see site
eligibility: PhD students actively enrolled at accredited institutions in eligible regions (incl. US), plus postdocs and faculty depending on the specific research challenge; eligibility varies per challenge — proposals must respond to a listed Microsoft Research challenge
description: Microsoft restructured its PhD Fellowship into a challenge-based fellowship: applicants propose work against specific Microsoft Research 'research challenges' and receive an unrestricted gift plus structured collaboration with MSR researchers.
relevanceToUser: Microsoft Research has major AI-for-science and health-futures groups, and challenge topics regularly include AI/ML; fit depends on the year's posted challenges, so check whether a health/AI or AI-for-science challenge is listed each fall. Open to PhD students AND postdocs/faculty, unlike most industry fellowships.

name: NVIDIA Graduate Fellowship
duration: 1 academic year (9 months), preceded by a required 3-4 month summer internship at NVIDIA
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2026-2027 cycle closed Sept 15, 2025 (3pm PT); next cycle expected summer 2026 with mid-September deadline
eligibility: Completed first year of PhD in CS, computer engineering, EE or related field; actively engaged in thesis research; must remain enrolled full-time through 2026-27 (no graduation before May/June 2027); must be available for mandatory in-person NVIDIA internship; award paid through the university with no overhead
description: Funds PhD students doing outstanding research relevant to accelerated computing, with particular interest in AI/ML, robotics and related areas, paired with an NVIDIA internship.
relevanceToUser: Deep-learning methods for oncology/genomics fit if framed around GPU-accelerated AI (NVIDIA has major healthcare/genomics efforts, e.g., Clara/BioNeMo); the mandatory internship and one-year term suit a mid-PhD student. Purely biological projects without a computing angle would not compete.

name: Request for Proposals: Global Health and Wellbeing in an Era of Transformative AI (plus rolling Technical AI Safety RFP)
careerStages: any (PIs at research institutions, nonprofits; individual researchers via institutions)
awardAmount: varies (grant-sized, project-dependent; the separate Technical AI Safety RFP has a ~$40M pool)
deadlineInfo: Rolling / open RFPs as of 2026 — see site; Coefficient Giving rebranded from Open Philanthropy in late 2025
eligibility: Nonprofits, universities and research institutions aligned with the RFP scope; the transformative-AI global health RFP explicitly welcomes biopharma pipeline research and work on how health R&D should respond to AI advances; no career-stage restriction
description: Open Philanthropy (now Coefficient Giving) runs open RFPs at the AI-health intersection, funding research on how AI transforms health R&D and biomedicine, alongside a large rolling technical AI safety RFP.
relevanceToUser: A plausible source of project funding for AI-accelerated biomedical/drug-pipeline research, though oriented to global-health impact rather than US blood-cancer research specifically — a proposal on AI methods accelerating therapeutic discovery could fit; less natural for disease-specific hematology work. Details could not be verified directly on the official page (access blocked), so treat amounts/deadlines as unconfirmed.

name: Kavli Foundation funding programs (incl. AI-for-Science Postdoctoral Fellowship with FutureHouse)
awardAmount: varies by program (e.g., Kavli Scholars $100,000/yr up to 2 yrs; Exploration Award up to $540,000/yr; KICK grants $10,000-$120,000)
deadlineInfo: Program-specific: 2026 AI-for-Science Postdoctoral Fellowship closed Feb 2026 (may recur); other 2026 deadlines Jul 22, Aug 3, Dec 15, 2026 for niche programs
eligibility: Most programs require Kavli Institute affiliation, invitation, or fit within Kavli's four fields (astrophysics, nanoscience, neuroscience, science & society); the AI-for-Science postdoc fellowship (with FutureHouse) funded AI-enabled neuroscience projects; no open-call cancer/oncology funding
description: Kavli funds basic science in astrophysics, nanoscience, neuroscience and science-and-society, largely through Kavli Institutes and targeted calls; it recently piloted an AI-for-Science postdoctoral fellowship in neuroscience with FutureHouse.
relevanceToUser: Weak fit for blood-cancer AI: Kavli does not fund oncology and most calls require Kavli Institute ties. Only worth watching if the AI-for-Science postdoc fellowship recurs with broader scope, or for nanomedicine-adjacent angles; deprioritize relative to the other programs on this list.

name: ARPA-H Open BAA — Mission Office Innovative Solution Openings (ISOs)
funder: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H)
awardAmount: varies (no fixed cap; scoped per proposed project/milestones)
deadlineInfo: Rolling / continuously open. Solution Summary may be submitted at any time; ARPA-H aims to give written feedback within ~30 business days; if encouraged, full proposal typically due ~45 calendar days after feedback. No proposers' day.
eligibility: All responsible sources: US and (per solicitation) non-US academia, nonprofits, for-profits, hospitals, small businesses, and individuals. SAM.gov registration with valid UEI required before proposal submission. Max 3 concurrent prime awards per entity.
description: ARPA-H replaced its single general Open BAA with four always-open Mission Office ISOs: Health Science Futures (HSF), Proactive Health (PHO), Resilient Systems (RSO), and Scalable Solutions (SSO). You pitch your own high-risk/high-reward concept via a short Solution Summary before writing a full proposal. Awards are usually Other Transactions (performance/milestone-based), not traditional grants.
relevanceToUser: This is the primary open mechanism where an AI/ML + computational-oncology concept (including blood-cancer modeling) can be proposed unsolicited. Health Science Futures explicitly targets 'modeling biology with AI platforms,' making it the best-fit office for a statistics/ML lab; Scalable Solutions and Resilient Systems suit AI tools for data/health-system scale.

name: IGoR — Intelligent Generator of Research
funder: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H)
awardAmount: see site (not published on program page)
deadlineInfo: Solicitation ARPA-H-SOL-26-155. Solution Summary was due June 25, 2026 12:00 PM ET (now passed); full proposals due Aug 13, 2026 12:00 PM ET for encouraged proposers only. Watch for amendments/re-opens.
eligibility: Teams spanning academic institutions, non-profits, and companies with expertise in computational biology, AI/ML systems, lab automation, protocol standardization, and experimental validation. Submit via ARPA-H Solutions Portal after SAM.gov registration.
description: IGoR aims to build an AI-driven research 'engine' that generates hypotheses, designs and runs experiments, and closes knowledge gaps: mechanistic disease models plus an AI orchestration layer that identifies missing information and recommends optimal experiments, coupled to automated labs.
relevanceToUser: Directly in the wheelhouse of an ML/statistics lab: the program centers on AI systems for experiment design, mechanistic modeling, and multimodal data integration. Blood-cancer biology could be a use case. Main caveat is timing — this cycle's Solution Summary window has closed, so treat as a target for a future amendment or as a teaming opportunity.

name: ADAPT — Advanced Analysis for Precision Cancer Therapy
funder: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H)
awardAmount: Up to $142 million (total program, across ~10 funded teams)
deadlineInfo: Already awarded — announced May 2025; ~10 teams funded across 3 technical areas. Not currently accepting new applications. Patient enrollment ongoing (EVOLVE breast, IMMUNO-BIOMAP lung, ASCEND-CRC colorectal). Watch for follow-on TAs.
eligibility: Was open to academia, industry, and integrated cancer centers as teams; now closed to new proposals. Included systems biology, bioinformatics, and mathematical-modeling performers.
description: ADAPT turns static, biomarker-guided cancer care into continuously adaptive treatment that tracks evolving tumor DNA/RNA/protein profiles in real time. Technical areas cover therapy-recommendation methods (multi-modal data fusion, 'early delta' biomarkers), evolutionary/adaptive platform trials, and a centralized real-time data/analytics platform.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant thematically (multi-modal data fusion, mathematical modeling, bioinformatics for adaptive therapy) even though it currently targets solid tumors and is closed. Useful as a model for what ARPA-H funds in computational oncology and as a potential collaboration/subaward or future re-compete to monitor; blood-cancer adaptation is a plausible follow-on.

name: ARPA-H Biomedical Data Fabric (BDF) Toolbox
funder: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H), in partnership with NCI
awardAmount: see site (not published; ~20 teams funded)
deadlineInfo: Already awarded — ~20 teams announced in 2024 via a Combined Module Announcement. Not currently open for new applications; monitor for phase 2 / follow-on modules.
eligibility: Was open to R&D performers in data integration/usability technologies; now closed. NCI CRDC/CBIIT are program partners.
description: BDF Toolbox is building an open-source system of unified data services to make heterogeneous biomedical datasets (from thousands of labs and clinics) interoperable and usable, including AI-assisted harmonization, clinical data integration, and patient digital-twin simulations. Many funded projects are cancer-focused.
relevanceToUser: Strongly aligned with AI/data-science methods for computational oncology (data harmonization, ML-ready cancer datasets, digital twins) and explicitly cancer-connected via NCI. Closed to new applicants, but relevant as landscape and as data/tooling infrastructure an ML lab could build on or partner into.

name: ARPA-H SBIR/STTR (FY2026 topics)
funder: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H)
awardAmount: Up to ~$600,000 (Phase I); up to ~$3.5 million (Phase II)
duration: Phase I short-term feasibility; Phase II multi-year development
deadlineInfo: FY2026 draft solicitation released with a two-step process (Solution Summary -> Technical Presentation); FY2026 Solution Summary due July 10, 2026 11:59 PM ET (now passed). New topic cycles recur — monitor SAM.gov and ARPA-H.
eligibility: US small businesses (<=500 employees), majority US-citizen/US-small-business owned, with all work performed in the US. Academic labs participate as subawardees/partners, not primes. Supports Phase I, Direct-to-Phase II, and Fast Track.
description: Non-dilutive small-business R&D funding across ~7 rotating health-innovation topics, including AI/predictive-model and computational-health themes. Two-phase structure with milestone-based contracts.
relevanceToUser: Only directly applicable if the researcher spins out or partners with a startup — a common path to commercialize AI/ML oncology tools. As an academic at your institution you would typically join as a subcontractor/PI-partner. Include as an option if there is a company vehicle; this cycle's window has closed, so aim at the next release.

name: NCI Informatics Technology for Cancer Research (ITCR) — Early-Stage / Advanced / Sustained Development
funder: National Cancer Institute (NCI), NIH (Cancer Moonshot / cancer data-science ecosystem)
awardAmount: Early-Stage (RFA-CA-27-019, U01): up to $300,000 direct costs/yr for up to 3 yrs (~$2.4M total, ~5 awards). Advanced (RFA-CA-27-020, U24) and Sustained (RFA-CA-27-021, U24): up to $600,000 direct costs/yr for up to 5 yrs.
deadlineInfo: Active for FY2027. Opened ~June 2026. Standard multi-cycle receipt dates (e.g., mid-June and mid-October cycles); RFA-CA-27-019 runs through 2027, RFA-CA-27-020/-021 expire ~Oct 2026. Verify exact dates in each RFA before applying.
eligibility: Open to higher-education institutions, nonprofits, for-profits, government, and (per NIH) foreign organizations; any qualified PD/PI. Requires SAM.gov, eRA Commons, and Grants.gov registration. Applications for ML/DL tools must include model cards (architecture, uses, bias/risks, training, evaluation).
description: ITCR funds development, hardening, and sustainment of informatics tools across the cancer research continuum. RFAs explicitly welcome 'novel machine learning/deep learning methods and models' for cancer data acquisition, management, and analysis, spanning early prototypes (U01) to widely adopted resources (U24).
relevanceToUser: Excellent fit for an AI/ML statistics lab building computational-oncology software: ML/DL methods are named as in-scope, and the early-stage U01 is well-suited to an early-career PI prototyping tools (including for leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS data). Cancer-specific, recurring, and investigator-initiated.

name: PRIMED-AI — Precision Medicine with AI: Integrating Imaging with Multimodal Data
awardAmount: Framework Playbook (RFA-RM-27-011, U01): up to ~$300,000 direct costs/yr. Data-to-Model and Model-to-Clinic partnerships (RFA-RM-27-012/-013, UG3/UH3), Validation Center (RFA-RM-27-014, U54), Logistics Center (RFA-RM-27-015, U24): amounts vary — see each RFA.
deadlineInfo: New program. RFAs opened ~June 30, 2026 with application due dates in October 2026 (e.g., ~Oct 2 for U54/U24 centers, ~Oct 9 for U01, ~Oct 19 for UG3/UH3). Currently OPEN and actionable. Confirm exact dates per RFA.
eligibility: Broad NIH eligibility (domestic and, per RFA, foreign institutions); mechanism-dependent. Some tracks require academic-industrial partnerships. Standard NIH registrations required.
description: A new NIH Common Fund initiative to build reliable, cost-effective AI tools that integrate clinical imaging with other multimodal health data for precision medicine, including standardized 'playbook' frameworks for responsible model use, error mitigation, data ontology/linkage, validation, and regulatory readiness.
relevanceToUser: Very strong fit for a multimodal-AI/ML lab: the program is explicitly about integrating imaging with multimodal data and building rigorous, deployable AI frameworks. Oncology (including hematologic malignancies with imaging + genomic + clinical data fusion) is squarely in scope, and it is open now with fall 2026 deadlines.

name: NCI Cancer Moonshot Scholars Program (R01)
funder: National Cancer Institute (NCI), NIH — Cancer Moonshot
awardAmount: R01-level (inaugural 2023 cohort: 11 scholars, ~$5.4M first-year total across awards); per-award varies — see current NOFO
deadlineInfo: Prior cycle was RFA-CA-22-050 (receipt dates 2022-2024, now expired). A current 2025-2026 reissue was not confirmed on an official page (program framing may have changed); verify current availability on grants.nih.gov and the NCI site before planning.
eligibility: Early-Stage Investigators (ESIs) seeking their first NCI R01. Any cancer-relevant topic, including AI/computational oncology, is allowable as the science. Confirm current eligibility language on the active NOFO.
description: A Cancer Moonshot early-career mechanism providing R01-level support to launch independent cancer research programs among early-stage investigators, explicitly aimed at broadening the NCI R01 talent pool.
relevanceToUser: The most on-point early-career-faculty pathway in the Cancer Moonshot cluster for someone transitioning to an independent AI/computational-oncology (including blood-cancer) research program. Caveat: the last published cycle has expired and a current reissue is unverified, so confirm before relying on it.

name: Damon Runyon Fellowship Award (Postdoctoral Fellowship)
awardAmount: ~$300,000 total over 4 years ($70K/$72K/$74K/$76K stipend by year = $292K + $2K/yr expense allowance = $8K)
deadlineInfo: Two cycles per year (spring ~mid-March and summer ~mid-August); next verified deadline Aug 14, 2026, 4:00pm ET. Via ProposalCentral.
eligibility: PhD/MD/MD-PhD/DDS/DVM/DO. Basic scientists: degree no more than 18 months before deadline and <=1 year in sponsor's lab. Physician-scientists: completed residency/clinical training, board-eligible, >=80% research effort, may apply until first assistant-professor appointment. Cannot have accepted another postdoc fellowship (T32 holders may apply but not hold both concurrently). Foreign nationals eligible if research performed in the US.
description: Flagship postdoctoral fellowship supporting the training of outstanding early-career postdocs in any area of cancer research, including theoretical/computational work.
