relevanceToUser: Open to any cancer-relevant research including computational/ML methods and blood-cancer projects; less prescriptive than the QB Fellowship but only 18 months post-PhD for basic scientists (tighter timing). Strong for a postdoc applying ML to leukemia/lymphoma/MDS genomics.

name: Damon Runyon-Rachleff Innovation Award
awardAmount: $200,000/year; $400,000 for Stage 1 (2 years), up to $800,000 total (up to 4 years) with Stage 2 approval
deadlineInfo: Annual; July 1 (next verified deadline July 1, 2026, 4:00pm ET). Two-stage review.
eligibility: Tenure-track Assistant Professor within first 5 years of appointment (or clinical instructor/senior clinical fellow in final year of subspecialty training, or distinguished fellow with dedicated lab space). MD/DO/PhD/MD-PhD. >=80% research effort. Non-US citizens eligible if research conducted at a US institution. Max 2 lifetime applications; no prior funding for the proposed project.
description: Supports early-career faculty with high-risk/high-reward ideas that lack the preliminary data needed for traditional (e.g., NIH) funding. Open to all scientific disciplines.
relevanceToUser: Good early-career-faculty mechanism if the researcher becomes an assistant professor. 'All scientific disciplines' means novel AI/ML approaches to blood cancers are eligible, provided they tie to cancer prevention/diagnosis/treatment. High-risk framing fits methods-development work without preliminary data.

name: Damon Runyon Clinical Investigator Award
awardAmount: $600,000 over 3 years ($200,000/yr to institution, no indirect costs) + up to $100,000 medical-school loan repayment (max $33,333/yr)
deadlineInfo: Annual; mid-January (next verified deadline Jan 19, 2027, 4:00pm ET). Institution-nominated; limit ~3 nominees per invited institution.
eligibility: MD, DO, or MD/PhD from an accredited institution; US board-eligible with active license; Assistant Professor (or equivalent) within 4 years of appointment; cannot hold an R01 or equivalent at time of application; >=80% research effort. Historically requires US citizenship/permanent residency; institution nomination required.
description: Supports early-career physician-scientists conducting innovative patient-oriented/clinical cancer research, with medical-school debt relief to enable a research career.
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the user is a physician-scientist (MD/DO). Blood-cancer clinical/translational work qualifies; computational analysis can be embedded, but this is a clinician award, not a methods/postdoc mechanism.

name: Damon Runyon Physician-Scientist Training Award
awardAmount: $460,000 over 4 years ($100K/$110K/$120K/$130K by year) + up to $100,000 medical-school loan repayment (max $25K/yr) + $1,000/child/yr dependent allowance
deadlineInfo: Annual; December 1 (next verified deadline Dec 1, 2026, 4:00pm ET). Via ProposalCentral.
eligibility: MD or DO only (MD/PhDs NOT eligible); must have completed residency and all clinical training; US board-eligible before start; <=3 years postdoctoral lab research experience; cannot be pursuing a PhD; cannot hold NIH K awards (institutional K12 must be relinquished). Up to 2 applications allowed.
description: Mentored training award to help newly trained physicians (MD/DO) launch cancer-research careers, with debt relief and family support.
relevanceToUser: Only for MD/DO physicians early in research training (not MD/PhDs or pure computational scientists). A hematology/oncology fellow doing computational blood-cancer research could qualify; not applicable to a PhD-track ML researcher.

name: Damon Runyon-St. Jude Pediatric Cancer Research Fellowship Award
awardAmount: see site (postdoctoral fellowship; figures not verified on official page during this review)
deadlineInfo: See site; aligns with the Damon Runyon Fellowship cycle. Verify current deadline on the award-programs page.
eligibility: Early-career postdoctoral scientists researching pediatric cancer prevention, diagnosis, or treatment. Verify degree/timing requirements on official page.
description: Postdoctoral fellowship (with St. Jude) dedicated to pediatric cancer research.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user's blood-cancer work targets pediatric leukemias/lymphomas. Computational/ML projects are compatible, but amounts/eligibility were not verifiable from an official page in this pass, so treat details as provisional.

name: Damon Runyon-Dale F. Frey Award for Breakthrough Scientists
awardAmount: see site (continuation/transition funding; figures not verified on official page during this review)
deadlineInfo: Not an open application; awarded by selection to current/recent Damon Runyon Fellows and Quantitative Biology Fellows.
eligibility: Restricted to outstanding current or recent Damon Runyon Fellows and Quantitative Biology Fellows transitioning to independent faculty positions. Not directly applyable by non-awardees.
description: Additional 'breakthrough' funding to help top-performing Damon Runyon/QB Fellows transition from postdoc to independent investigator.
relevanceToUser: Only relevant as a downstream trajectory award IF the user first wins a Damon Runyon Fellowship or QB Fellowship; cannot be applied to directly now. Included for landscape completeness.

name: CRI Immuno-Informatics Postdoctoral Fellowship
awardAmount: $243,000 total over 3 years (stipend $74K/$76K/$78K by year = $228K + $5K/yr institutional allowance = $15K)
deadlineInfo: Two cycles annually; March 1 and September 1, 11:59pm ET (verified 2026-2027 cycle). Decisions in ~10-12 weeks.
eligibility: Doctoral degree by activation; <5 years mentored research experience (residency excluded for MDs); no citizenship restrictions (US or abroad, non-profit institutions only). Projects must combine immunology/cancer immunology with data science, using innovative computational methods, high-throughput 'omics, and advanced data analysis.
description: Postdoctoral fellowship for scientists seeking dual training in immunology and data science - supporting computational biologists deepening immunology, or immunologists gaining computational/genomics expertise.
relevanceToUser: Excellent fit for an AI/ML researcher pivoting into cancer immunology, and directly applicable to blood-cancer immunotherapy (CAR-T, immune profiling of leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma). No citizenship restriction. Requires an immunology angle to the computational work.

name: CRI Irvington Postdoctoral Fellowship
awardAmount: $243,000 total over 3 years (stipend $74K/$76K/$78K by year = $228K + $5K/yr institutional allowance = $15K)
deadlineInfo: Two cycles annually; March 1 and September 1, 11:59pm ET (verified). Activation 4-12 months after deadline.
eligibility: Doctoral degree by activation; <5 years mentored research experience (residency excluded for MDs); no citizenship restrictions; non-profit institutions (US or abroad). Project must fall within immunology relevant to cancer, emphasizing hypothesis-driven, mechanistic tumor/fundamental immunology.
description: CRI's flagship postdoctoral fellowship for fundamental and tumor immunology research relevant to cancer.
relevanceToUser: Applicable if the computational work is framed as mechanistic cancer immunology (the Immuno-Informatics track is the more data-science-native sibling). Blood-cancer immunology projects qualify. No citizenship restriction.

name: CRI Technology Impact Award
awardAmount: Up to $200,000 seed funding (over 12-24 months)
deadlineInfo: Two-step; Letter of Intent due ~November 15, full application (by invitation) due ~March 13. Earliest activation July 1.
eligibility: Tenure-track position (or higher) at a non-profit research center/institution. No citizenship restriction. Supports computational systems-biology approaches to large-scale datasets and predictive modeling of therapy response/resistance.
description: Seed grant funding development/application of enabling technologies - explicitly including computational systems biology and modeling - to advance cancer immunotherapy.
relevanceToUser: Strongly computational: designed for methods/modeling that analyze large datasets and predict immunotherapy response/resistance. Relevant for a faculty-level ML researcher building predictive models in cancer (adaptable to hematologic malignancy immunotherapy). Requires a faculty (tenure-track) appointment.

name: CRI Clinic and Laboratory Integration Program (CLIP) Grant
awardAmount: $300,000 over 2 years (renewable for a second $300,000/2-year round)
deadlineInfo: Letter of Intent due ~December 1; full proposal (by invitation) due ~March 12; earliest activation July 1.
eligibility: Tenure-track Assistant Professor (or higher) at award activation; no citizenship restriction; research at non-profit institutions (US or abroad); for-profit institutions excluded.
description: Project grant bridging laboratory immunology and clinical practice - translating basic discoveries toward patient therapies and using clinical observations to drive fundamental immunotherapy research.
relevanceToUser: Faculty-level translational immunotherapy grant. A computational researcher could lead or co-lead a CLIP project analyzing immune/clinical datasets from blood-cancer immunotherapy trials, but it requires a tenure-track appointment and a clear clinic-lab translational bridge.

name: CRI Lloyd J. Old STAR Program (Scientists Taking Risks)
awardAmount: $1,250,000 unrestricted over 5 years (+ up to 10% institutional indirect costs)
deadlineInfo: Annual; ~January 15, 11:59pm ET (next verified deadline Jan 15, 2027). Interviews in spring; earliest activation July 1.
eligibility: MD, PhD, or MD/PhD (or equivalent); tenure-track Assistant Professor with >=3 years in position, OR Associate Professor with <=3 years in position; >=80% research effort; no citizenship restriction (US or abroad, excluding for-profit); adjunct/acting positions ineligible.
description: Flexible, investigator-based (not project-based) funding for mid-career scientists pursuing high-risk, high-reward cancer immunotherapy research.
relevanceToUser: For established/mid-career faculty in cancer immunotherapy. Unrestricted funding suits a computational immunology program (e.g., ML for immune monitoring in leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma), but the career stage (3+ years as assistant professor) puts it beyond a postdoc/PhD applicant.

name: V Scholar Cancer Research Grant
funder: The V Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $800,000 over 4 years ($200,000/yr, no indirect costs)
deadlineInfo: Closed/nomination-based (one nominee per invited institution). 2026 cycle: institution LOI mid-May, nomination to V Foundation ~June 1, full application ~June 30. Annual.
eligibility: Tenure-track (or tenured) Assistant Professor within first 5 years of first appointment, not yet promoted to Associate; US citizen or lawful permanent resident (also invited Canadian institutions); >=2 years postdoctoral (MD/PhD) training (1 yr acceptable for some MD specialties). Excluded if prior V Scholar, or if holding an R01/R01-equivalent, or any non-mentored single grant >=$750K or aggregate non-mentored funding >$1M. Adult cancers only (excludes pediatric, epidemiology, behavioral, health-services research).
description: Early-career-faculty grant to launch independent adult-cancer research programs (fundamental or translational). A detailed biostatistics/bioinformatics plan is mandatory for every proposal.
relevanceToUser: Well-suited to a computationally driven blood-cancer program: any adult cancer type qualifies (leukemia, lymphoma, myeloma, MDS) and a bioinformatics/biostatistics plan is explicitly required, so ML/statistical methods are welcomed. Key constraints: must be a tenure-track Assistant Professor at an invited institution (your institution-type institutions are typically eligible but must be invited) and a US citizen/permanent resident; not for postdocs or trainees.

name: V Foundation Translational Grant
funder: The V Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $200,000/year (typically $800,000 over 4 years; confirm total on current RFA)
deadlineInfo: Nomination/invitation-based via NCI-designated and other invited institutions; spring cycle similar to V Scholar. Confirm current dates on official site.
eligibility: Faculty investigators pursuing translational (lab-to-clinic) cancer research at invited institutions. Specific career-stage and citizenship requirements per RFA (generally US citizen/permanent resident; institution nomination required).
description: Supports translational cancer research moving laboratory findings toward clinical trials/patient impact.
relevanceToUser: Relevant for faculty doing translational blood-cancer work with a computational component (e.g., biomarker/response models feeding a trial). Like V Scholar, it is nomination-based and faculty-level, not a trainee mechanism; verify exact amount/eligibility on the current RFA.

name: V Foundation All-Star Grant
funder: The V Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $1,000,000 over 5 years
deadlineInfo: By invitation only; reinvestment in prior high-performing V Foundation awardees. Terms per RFA.
eligibility: Restricted to previous V Scholar, Translational, or Game-Changer recipients who are invited to apply. Not open to first-time applicants.
description: Reinvestment grant giving larger, longer funding to previously funded V Foundation investigators with strong track records.
relevanceToUser: Only a downstream option IF the user first receives a V Scholar/Translational/Game-Changer award; cannot be applied to directly. Included for landscape completeness.

name: IMS and Paula and Rodger Riney Foundation Translational Research Grant (IMS Translational Research Award)
funder: International Myeloma Society (co-funded by the Paula and Rodger Riney Foundation)
awardAmount: $175,000–$250,000 USD total (includes up to 8% institutional overhead); second-year funding available based on progress
duration: 1 year, renewable to a 2nd year contingent on progress
deadlineInfo: Applications due January 30, 2026 at 11:59 PM EST (6th annual cycle); recurs annually in late January
eligibility: Must be a faculty member or scientist with an MD and/or PhD who is established as a researcher; the PI or a Co-Investigator must be an IMS member. Open to researchers from outside plasma cell malignancies who wish to apply their expertise (e.g., computational/AI methods) to myeloma. Women and underrepresented minorities encouraged. Not restricted to US citizens, but a US-based researcher is eligible.
description: Investigator-initiated translational research grant to advance prevention, early detection, diagnosis, and treatment of plasma cell disorders (multiple myeloma), spanning molecular assays, imaging techniques, drugs, and biological agents moving toward clinical translation. Jointly funded by IMS and the Riney Foundation.
relevanceToUser: The most substantial myeloma-specific grant here and explicitly open to investigators from adjacent fields refocusing on myeloma — a strong fit for an ML/computational scientist proposing AI-driven biomarker discovery, imaging, or genomic-targeting work in myeloma, provided the applicant (or a Co-I) holds faculty/scientist status and joins IMS. Requires a translational angle tied to clinical impact.

name: IMS Career Development Award
funder: International Myeloma Society (co-funded by the Paula and Rodger Riney Foundation)
focusAreas: computational biology/oncology (all areas of plasma cell dyscrasias eligible)
awardAmount: Up to $75,000 USD (includes up to 8% institutional overhead); covers up to 1 year salary support, plus a travel/visa allowance and 1 year complimentary IMS membership
deadlineInfo: Applications due January 30, 2026 at 11:59 PM EST (6th annual cycle); recurs annually in late January
eligibility: Must hold an MD or PhD (or be in the process of obtaining a PhD in plasma cell dyscrasias), focus research on plasma cell dyscrasias, not yet be an independent researcher, plan a career in plasma cell disorders, and have never received NIH funding or an international equivalent. Cannot already be employed by the host laboratory. Women and underrepresented minorities encouraged.
description: Young-investigator career-development award to foster academic careers and collaborative research in plasma cell disorders; all areas of plasma cell dyscrasias research are eligible. Jointly funded by IMS and the Riney Foundation.
relevanceToUser: Well-suited to a myeloma-focused PhD student or postdoc in a computational/ML lab who has not yet received NIH funding and is establishing independence. The 'no prior NIH funding' and 'not yet independent' criteria make it a genuine early-career entry point for computational myeloma research; the salary support can fund a trainee's myeloma project.

name: IMS Young Investigator Award (travel grant for exemplary abstracts)
awardAmount: Up to $2,000 USD travel grant to offset costs of attending the IMS Annual Meeting
duration: One-time travel award tied to the Annual Meeting
deadlineInfo: Tied to IMS Annual Meeting abstract submission; specific deadline not published on the award page — see site / abstract portal
eligibility: Age 35 years or younger, registered for the IMS Annual Meeting, and primary author of an abstract selected for oral or poster presentation. Selection is made by Annual Meeting abstract reviewers and the IMS Executive Committee.
description: Merit-based travel grant recognizing the best abstracts by young myeloma researchers (primary authors) at the IMS Annual Meeting.
relevanceToUser: A low-effort, meeting-linked award for a trainee presenting computational/AI myeloma work at the IMS Annual Meeting; useful for travel funding and visibility rather than research support. Age cap of 35 applies.

name: Paula and Rodger Riney Foundation Direct / Investigator-Initiated Multiple Myeloma Grants
awardAmount: Varies — the Foundation makes direct grants and gifts ranging from ~$1M to $40M to institutions and programs (e.g., $40M to Dana-Farber, $5.9M to Medical College of Wisconsin, $1M to Blood Cancer United); no fixed per-investigator amount published
deadlineInfo: No published open application cycle or deadline; proposals are reviewed on a rolling basis by direct contact with the Foundation
eligibility: Open to multiple myeloma researchers and medical institutions; the Foundation 'carefully reviews and provides grants and gifts directly to worthy programs and projects proposed by multiple myeloma researchers and institutions.' No public eligibility rubric — typically routed through an institution or cancer-center development office.
description: Beyond its co-funded IMS awards, the Foundation makes direct, investigator- and institution-initiated grants for multiple myeloma research, historically large strategic gifts supporting novel CAR-T, immunotherapy, drug-resistance, and genomic-targeting work at major cancer centers.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the user's myeloma/computational work sits within a major cancer center that could partner with the Foundation, but there is no open individual application portal — access is typically via an institutional introduction (contact P.O. Box 31729, St. Louis, MO 63131). Best pursued through a myeloma PI or the institution's development office rather than as a standalone fellowship. Amount and terms unverified for individual applicants.

name: Young Investigator Award (YIA) — General Competition
funder: Conquer Cancer, the ASCO Foundation (American Society of Clinical Oncology)
careerStages: clinician-scientist (final years of oncology fellowship/subspecialty training)
awardAmount: $50,000 total (one year); supports personnel/research expenses plus travel to the ASCO Annual Meeting grants ceremony
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle; 2026 full application was due ~January 29, 2026 (11:59 PM ET). Next (2027) cycle deadline not yet published — see site.
eligibility: Must be a physician (MD, DO, or international equivalent); MD/PhD eligible if both degrees complete before grant start. Must be within the last two years of final oncology subspecialty training/fellowship at an academic institution at application. Active medical license required. ASCO membership required (or applied for). Must commit ≥60% effort to research. Open to applicants in any country. NOTE: requires a medical/physician degree — a PhD-only computational researcher is not directly eligible but could serve as a collaborator/co-mentor.
description: One-year mentored seed grant helping physician-scientists at the cusp of their first faculty appointment launch an independent oncology research direction. Research area is open, including translational and data-driven projects.
relevanceToUser: Directly funds early-stage oncology research and explicitly welcomes translational/data projects; blood-cancer topics are eligible. Best fit if the user collaborates with (or is) a physician-scientist, since the mechanism requires an MD/DO. A computational/AI researcher could partner with a fellow applicant as mentor/co-investigator on an ML-driven leukemia/lymphoma project.

name: Young Investigator Award (YIA) — Special/Disease-Specific Competitions (incl. Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia and Mantle Cell Lymphoma)
awardAmount: $50,000 total (one year), same as general YIA; some competitions are endowment-funded and may vary — see site
deadlineInfo: Shares the YIA application portal and cycle; individual competitions open/close on their own timelines (e.g., the 2026 CLL and Mantle Cell Lymphoma competitions have closed). Check the special-competitions page each cycle.
eligibility: Same core criteria as the general YIA (physician/MD-DO, within last two years of oncology subspecialty training, ≥60% research effort, ASCO membership). Applicants to a disease-specific competition may also be considered for general YIA funding. Requires a medical degree.
description: Disease-earmarked versions of the YIA funded by dedicated philanthropy. Recent hematologic-malignancy competitions include Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) and Mantle Cell Lymphoma, directly targeting blood cancers.
relevanceToUser: The most blood-cancer-targeted ASCO early-career mechanism: recent cycles funded CLL and mantle cell lymphoma specifically. Highly relevant thematically to leukemia/lymphoma AI work, though eligibility is limited to physician-scientists, so a computational researcher would apply through a clinical collaborator.

name: AACR-Conquer Cancer, the ASCO Foundation Young Investigator Award for Translational Cancer Research
funder: American Association for Cancer Research (AACR) in partnership with Conquer Cancer, the ASCO Foundation
careerStages: clinician-scientist transitioning from fellowship to faculty
awardAmount: $50,000 total (one year), grant term starting July 1, 2026
deadlineInfo: Annual; 2026 cycle submission deadline was December 18, 2025 (1:00 PM ET) via ProposalCentral; decisions ~February 2026. Next cycle typically opens fall — see site.
eligibility: Medical degree required (MD, DO, MD/PhD, or equivalent); doctoral degree no earlier than July 1, 2016 (within 10 years of award start); must be in last two years of final subspecialty training; active medical license; ≥60% full-time research effort; active AACR membership; mentor at sponsoring institution. Research may be in ANY area of translational cancer research (computational/data-driven projects are not excluded).
description: A translational-research-focused version of the YIA co-administered by AACR, supporting physician-scientists transitioning from fellowship to faculty. Open to any translational cancer research area.
relevanceToUser: Explicitly open to any translational research area, which can accommodate AI/ML and computational methods applied to blood cancers. Requires a medical degree, so best pursued with or by a physician-scientist collaborator.

name: Career Development Award (CDA)
focusAreas: computational oncology (as part of translational work)
careerStages: physician-scientist (1st-4th year of first faculty appointment)
awardAmount: $200,000 total (plus up to $1,500 travel). Duration is listed as 3 years on ASCO's official description; some grant listings state 2 years ($100,000/year) — confirm current terms on the official page.
duration: 3 years (per ASCO; some sources list 2 years — see site)
deadlineInfo: Annual; 2026 general-competition full application deadline reported as ~March 26, 2026; decisions ~3 months after deadline. Verify current-cycle date on the official portal.
eligibility: Physician (MD, DO, or international equivalent) in the 1st-4th year of a full-time primary faculty appointment in a clinical department at an academic medical institution; within 10 years of the medical degree; pursuing an independent clinical-investigator career; ≥50% research effort; ASCO membership; institutional mentor; active medical license. Research must be patient-oriented (clinical study and/or translational research involving human subjects). Can hold concurrent K23/K08/K12/T32-type awards if no scientific/budget overlap. NOTE: requires a physician degree.
description: Three-year grant helping newly appointed faculty clinical investigators establish an independent, patient-oriented cancer research program; larger and later-stage than the YIA.
relevanceToUser: A strong early-career-faculty mechanism for patient-oriented oncology research, and can support translational/computational studies in hematologic malignancies. Eligibility is limited to physician-scientists in their first faculty years; a computational/ML faculty member without an MD would participate as a co-investigator rather than PI.

name: Hope Funds for Cancer Research Postdoctoral Fellowship
funder: The Hope Funds for Cancer Research
focusAreas: computational biology (eligible if aligned with focus)
careerStages: PhD student transitioning to postdoc (may apply before starting)
awardAmount: 3-year fellowship: $56,000 (yr 1), $59,000 (yr 2), $62,000 (yr 3) stipend, plus $1,500/year travel grant
deadlineInfo: Annual; Letter of Intent/abstract due ~September 24, full application due ~January 25 of the following year. (Cutoff for prior lab time was Sept 24, 2025 for the current cycle.) Verify next-cycle dates on the official site.
eligibility: Must hold MD, PhD, MD/PhD, DDS, or DVM (or foreign-physician equivalent, case-by-case). May apply before or during a postdoc; if already appointed, must have been in the sponsor's lab ≤2 years as of the LOI deadline. Research must be conducted at a US university, hospital, or nonprofit research institution (no for-profit institutions). Cannot have already accepted another postdoctoral fellowship award.
description: Prestigious 3-year postdoctoral fellowship for innovative, paradigm-challenging research on difficult-to-treat cancers — explicitly including rare leukemias, lymphomas, and MDS.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant: PhD-holders are eligible (unlike the ASCO physician-only awards), and the funded scope explicitly names rare leukemias, lymphomas, and MDS — squarely matching the user's blood-cancer focus. Innovative computational/AI approaches to blood-cancer biology fit the 'paradigm-challenging' mandate, provided the work is done at a US nonprofit institution. This is the best direct fit for a computational postdoc.

name: Pershing Square Sohn Prize for Young Investigators in Cancer Research
funder: Pershing Square Sohn Cancer Research Alliance (The Pershing Square Foundation)
awardAmount: $250,000 per year for 3 years ($750,000 total)
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. For the 2027 Prize: application portal opens Sept 21, 2026; Letter of Intent due Oct 26, 2026 (5pm ET); applicants notified mid-Feb 2027; finalist presentations Mar/Apr 2027 in NYC; winners announced mid-April 2027; projects begin July 1, 2027. Minimum of 10 prizes awarded annually.
eligibility: PI must hold an MD, PhD, or MD-PhD (or equivalent) and a faculty appointment at the Assistant or Associate Professor level (or equivalent) at a US academic research institution. Must have run their own independent lab for at least 2 years and no more than 6 years by the award start date. Cancer-research background is NOT required, but the setting must be capable of high-quality cancer research. Non-US citizens must confirm their visa allows completing the project in the US. Applicants may apply at most twice total.
description: A prize for rising-star early-career faculty pursuing bold, innovative cancer research. Research areas may include basic research and technological/computational applications, with no requirement for a prior cancer-research background.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant and newly accessible: as of the 2026/2027 cycle the prize expanded from the NYC metro area to ALL US institutions, so an your institution statistics/ML faculty member now qualifies. It explicitly welcomes technological and methods-driven applications (AI/ML), does not require a cancer-research pedigree, and would fund computational-oncology work on leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma once the applicant is 2-6 years into an independent faculty appointment. Physician-scientists (MD-PhD) are also eligible.

name: Stand Up To Cancer (SU2C) Innovative Research Grants (IRG)
funder: Stand Up To Cancer (administered by the American Association for Cancer Research, AACR)
awardAmount: Up to $750,000 over the grant term (historical; verify current RFA)
deadlineInfo: RFA-based, not a fixed annual cycle. SU2C opens Requests For Applications as funds become available. As of mid-2026 there is NO open IRG application; the AACR program page reflects a prior cycle. Historically: Letter of Intent (fall) followed by invited full proposals via proposalCENTRAL, with ~10 awards made. Monitor the SU2C grant-opportunities page and @SU2CScience.
eligibility: Tenure-track (or equivalent) independent investigators, historically within ~7 years of starting their first independent faculty position, holding a medical or other doctoral degree, at US academic/medical/research institutions. Postdocs and researchers-in-training are ineligible; government and for-profit employees cannot be PI; SU2C Dream Team Leaders/Principals are ineligible. No citizenship/residency restriction. (Verify exact windows against the active RFA when reopened.)
description: Funds novel, high-risk/high-reward cancer research across basic, translational, or clinical disciplines with strong potential for patient impact. Aimed at independent early-career investigators.
relevanceToUser: A strong fit for high-risk/high-reward computational-oncology ideas once the researcher holds an independent faculty position (within ~7 years). Any cancer discipline is eligible, so AI/ML methods applied to blood cancers would qualify. Caveat: it is episodic/RFA-driven rather than continuously open, so timing must be watched.

name: SU2C Convergence Research Teams / Dream Team Grants (incl. Machine Learning Convergence Research Team)
funder: Stand Up To Cancer (administered by AACR)
awardAmount: varies (multimillion-dollar multi-institution team grants; Dream Teams historically ~$10M+ over 3-4 years; Convergence teams smaller — see site)
deadlineInfo: RFA-based, opens as funds become available; applications are team-based (multi-institution collaborations), often assembled by nomination/idea sessions rather than open individual application. No open call listed as of mid-2026; check the SU2C grant-opportunities page.
eligibility: Team-based grants for collaborations of investigators across institutions; SU2C Convergence explicitly integrates medical, physical, and computational scientists (data scientists, ML/AI researchers). Individual computational researchers typically join as co-investigators/team members rather than sole PI. US-based institutions; specifics vary by RFA.
description: Large collaborative team grants (Dream Teams and Convergence Research Teams) that unite clinical, biological, physical, and computational scientists on major cancer challenges. SU2C Convergence deliberately embeds data science, machine learning, and NLP expertise (e.g., the Machine Learning for Immunotherapy Convergence Research Team).
relevanceToUser: The most natural SU2C route for an AI/ML researcher: Convergence teams are explicitly built to bring machine-learning and computational scientists onto cancer teams, and a statistics/ML lab could join as the computational engine of a Dream/Convergence team (including one focused on hematologic malignancies). Not a solo grant, so best pursued by partnering with clinical/biology collaborators when an RFA opens.

name: SU2C Phillip A. Sharp Innovation in Collaboration Awards
funder: Stand Up To Cancer (administered by AACR)
awardAmount: Approximately $200,000-$250,000 per team (historical, 2014-2020, ~$1M total per cohort); see site to confirm current amount
deadlineInfo: Awarded annually to scientists within the SU2C research community; short (historically ~250-word) proposals with rapid review. No open public application cycle indicated; confirm current status with AACR/SU2C.
eligibility: Restricted to scientists already within the SU2C research community (members of existing SU2C Dream Teams / funded teams) who propose new cross-team collaborations. NOT an open award for outside applicants who have never been SU2C-funded.
description: Seed grants that fund novel collaborations between scientists on different SU2C teams, named after Nobel laureate Phillip A. Sharp to reward cross-team innovation.
relevanceToUser: Only relevant once the researcher is already part of the SU2C ecosystem (e.g., on a Dream Team or Convergence team). It is not an entry-point grant for a new external applicant, but it is worth knowing as a follow-on funding route if the lab joins an SU2C team. Included for completeness of the SU2C cluster.

name: Sidney Kimmel Foundation Kimmel Scholar Award (DISCONTINUED)
funder: Sidney Kimmel Foundation for Cancer Research
awardAmount: $200,000 total (historical; ~$100,000/year over 2 years). Program no longer awarding.
deadlineInfo: CLOSED / DISCONTINUED. The program ran 1997-2017; the 2017 class was the final class of recipients. No new applications are possible in 2025-2026. The foundation has redirected funds (e.g., to the Bloomberg~Kimmel Institute for Immunotherapy at Johns Hopkins).
eligibility: Historical: newly independent investigators at the assistant-professor level (or equivalent) engaged in basic, clinical, or translational cancer research, holding an MD, PhD, or equivalent, at US not-for-profit institutions. No longer applicable — program is closed.
description: A former early-career award that jump-started the careers of ~15 promising cancer researchers/physician-scientists per year (~290 scholars, >$65M committed over 20 years). The program is now permanently closed.
relevanceToUser: Would have been an excellent early-career-faculty fit, but it is DISCONTINUED and cannot be applied to. Flagged so the researcher does not spend effort on it and instead redirects to active analogues (e.g., Pershing Square Sohn Prize, Damon Runyon, or NCI K/DP2 mechanisms). Included to correct any outdated list that still shows it as open.

name: Career Awards for Medical Scientists (CAMS)
awardAmount: $700,000 total over five years
duration: 5 years (optional postdoctoral phase up to 2 years plus faculty phase)
deadlineInfo: Full proposal due Sep 30, 2026 at 3:00 PM EDT; award start Sep 1, 2027 (end Aug 31, 2032). Annual cycle. ~12% success rate historically.
eligibility: Requires a clinical doctorate (MD, DO, DDS, DVM, or equivalent). PhD-only or PharmD-only candidates are INELIGIBLE. Must be a postdoctoral researcher or pre-tenure faculty who has NOT accepted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position at application; committed to an academic research career with 75% time devoted to research; at least one first-author peer-reviewed publication (most competitive have several) and ~2+ years research experience, up to ~2 years from independence. Based at a US or Canadian non-profit degree-granting institution, research institute, or affiliated teaching hospital. Cannot hold concurrent BWF award, NIH DP5, or NIH K99/R00.
description: BWF's flagship physician-scientist bridging award: $700K over five years to help clinically trained researchers span the gap between advanced postdoctoral/fellowship training and the early independent-faculty years, at the interface of clinical practice and biomedical science.
relevanceToUser: This is the marquee bridging award for physician-scientists doing patient-oriented research, and blood-cancer / hematologic-malignancy work is squarely in scope. Important caveat for this user: CAMS requires a clinical degree (MD/DO/etc.) and explicitly excludes PhD-only applicants, so a computational/ML PhD is not eligible on their own — it is directly relevant only if the user is an MD-PhD / physician-scientist, or as the target award for an MD collaborator on their AI + computational oncology projects. (For the PhD-only computational profile, BWF CASI below is the equivalent bridging mechanism.)

name: Postdoctoral Diversity Enrichment Program (PDEP)
awardAmount: $60,000 total over three years (enrichment/career-development funds; cannot cover salary or indirect costs)
deadlineInfo: Application deadline Jan 20, 2026; notice of award Jun 15, 2026; award start Sep 1, 2026 (end Aug 31, 2029). Annual cycle.
eligibility: Must be a US or Canadian citizen who is a member of an underrepresented group (American Indian or Alaska Native, Black or African American, Hispanic, or Native Hawaiian or other Pacific Islander). Must have secured a funded postdoctoral position at a US or Canadian degree-granting institution, with no more than 48 months of postdoctoral research experience and no more than 5 years from the PhD at application. Research must be in the biomedical or medical sciences.
description: Career-development supplement ($60K over three years for research supplies, training, and enrichment) for postdoctoral fellows from underrepresented groups pursuing careers in biomedical or medical research.
relevanceToUser: Applicable if the user is at the postdoc stage and meets BWF's underrepresented-group and citizenship criteria; computational oncology / blood-cancer research qualifies as biomedical research. It funds enrichment/career development rather than direct salary, so it complements (rather than replaces) a primary research grant. (Already noted as present in the master list; re-verified with current cycle details.)

name: NIH Director's New Innovator Award (DP2)
funder: NIH Common Fund (Office of the Director)
awardAmount: $475,000 direct costs/year for 5 years (~$2.375M direct total), plus standard indirect costs. Note: the FY2025 cycle used a two-segment structure of $900K direct over 3 years + $600K direct over 2 years.
deadlineInfo: Annual. FY2026 cycle (RFA-RM-27-002) applications due August 17, 2026; Grants.gov opens ~July prior. New cycle each year.
eligibility: Must be an NIH Early Stage Investigator: completed terminal research degree or postgraduate clinical training within the past 10 years and never received a substantial NIH independent research award (e.g., R01). Must hold an independent research position at a U.S. institution by the award start. Single PD/PI only. Min 25% (3 person-months) effort. No preliminary data required.
description: High-Risk, High-Reward mechanism supporting exceptionally creative early-career investigators pursuing bold, highly innovative research with potential for major impact. Emphasis is on the innovativeness of the investigator and idea rather than a detailed project plan.
relevanceToUser: Ideal for an early-independent faculty member launching a lab around novel AI/ML methods for computational oncology and blood cancers. The award explicitly rewards bold, unconventional methodological approaches and does not require preliminary data or disease-specific framing, so an ML-methods program applied to leukemia/lymphoma/MDS fits well.

name: NIH Director's Pioneer Award (DP1)
funder: NIH Common Fund (Office of the Director)
awardAmount: $700,000 direct costs/year for each of 5 years (~$3.5M direct total), plus standard indirect costs.
deadlineInfo: Annual. Current cycle RFA-RM-27-001 applications due September 9, 2026. New cycle each year.
eligibility: Open to scientists at any career stage with an outstanding record of creativity. Single PD/PI only. The proposed work must take a new research direction substantially different from the applicant's (or others') current work. Recipients must commit the major portion of effort (>6 person-months / >=51%) in the first 3 years. U.S. institution. No detailed experimental plan or budget expected; core is a 5-page essay.
description: The flagship High-Risk, High-Reward award for pioneering, paradigm-shifting research with unusually broad scientific impact. Judged largely on the investigator's creativity and the transformative potential of a new research direction.
relevanceToUser: Suited to an established or ambitious mid-career researcher proposing a genuinely new AI/ML research direction in cancer biology. The essay-based, plan-light format rewards visionary computational approaches; a pivot from prior work into a bold ML-for-oncology program is exactly the profile Pioneer seeks.

name: NCI Outstanding Investigator Award (R35)
awardAmount: Up to $600,000 direct costs per year for 7 years, plus indirect costs.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle (typically one fall due date). Most recent announcement RFA-CA-22-045 (final due dates through fall 2025); reissuance expected — confirm current RFA/deadline on the NCI OIA site.
eligibility: Institution-nominated PD/PI with an outstanding record of cancer research over the last ~5 consecutive years, and continuous NCI research-grant funding (eligible mechanisms include R01, R35, R37, P01, P50, U01, U54, DP1, DP2, etc.) in each fiscal year over the qualifying window. At least 6 person-months effort. NCI encourages nomination of meritorious mid-career, women, and under-represented-minority candidates.
description: Provides long-term, flexible support to accomplished cancer researchers with outstanding records, funding a research program (not a single project) to enable ambitious, long-horizon oncology science.
relevanceToUser: The premier established-investigator award specifically for cancer research, directly on-target for a computational oncology / blood-cancer program. Requires an existing NCI funding track record, so it is a later-career target rather than an entry point, but 7 years of stable funding is uniquely valuable for methods-heavy ML work.

name: NIH Director's Transformative Research Award (TR01 / R01)
funder: NIH Common Fund (Office of the Director)
awardAmount: No fixed budget cap — budgets are flexible up to the maximum funds available for the initiative (varies; large budgets need no prior approval).
deadlineInfo: Annual. Recent cycle announced under RFA-RM-25-003; confirm the current RFA and due date on the program page (typically one submission per year).
eligibility: Open to all career stages; supports individuals or teams (single- or multi-PI). Projects must be inherently risky, untested, and capable of creating or overturning fundamental paradigms. No preliminary data required. U.S. institutions.
description: High-Risk, High-Reward award for exceptionally innovative, unconventional, paradigm-shifting projects. Uniquely allows teams and flexible/large budgets, making it suited to large-scale, ambitious efforts.
relevanceToUser: Fits a large, potentially collaborative AI/ML effort in oncology (e.g., a new computational framework spanning multiple blood-cancer datasets). The team-eligible, uncapped-budget structure is well suited to data- and compute-intensive ML programs that exceed a standard R01 scope.

name: NIH Director's Early Independence Award (DP5)
funder: NIH Common Fund (Office of the Director)
awardAmount: Up to $350,000 direct costs per year for 5 years, plus standard indirect costs (some prior cycles listed ~$250,000/year — confirm current figure).
deadlineInfo: Annual, but note: the FY2026 Early Independence Award opportunity will NOT go forward; a new opportunity is anticipated for FY2027. Most recent completed cycle was RFA-RM-24-005. Check the program page before planning.
eligibility: For exceptional junior scientists who bypass the postdoc: applicant must be within 16 months after (or 12 months before) completing their terminal research degree or postgraduate clinical training at the submission date, and hold a non-independent position at submission. Single PI only; institutions limited to 2 applications. Requires ~9.6 person-months effort in first 2 years; 3–5 reference letters. No preliminary data required.
description: High-Risk, High-Reward mechanism that lets outstanding new PhDs skip the postdoc and move directly into an independent research position with 5 years of funding.
relevanceToUser: Highly relevant for a graduating PhD / very recent PhD from a strong ML lab who wants to launch an independent computational-oncology group immediately without a postdoc. The tight post-degree window is the key constraint, and the FY2026 pause means timing must be checked carefully.

name: NHLBI Outstanding Investigator Award (OIA) (R35)
funder: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI), NIH
awardAmount: Varies — budget is based on the funding level of the PI's current NHLBI research projects, up to the NOFO maximum (final amount set by merit/available funds). See site.
deadlineInfo: Current cycle RFA-HL-26-002; application due date February 19, 2026. Reissued periodically.
eligibility: Established investigators with at least four consecutive years of active NHLBI R01-equivalent funding. Single-PI applications; minimum 50% research effort. Supports a research program spanning NHLBI's mission (heart, lung, blood, sleep).
description: Long-term, flexible R35 program support for accomplished NHLBI-funded investigators, funding a research program rather than a single project.
relevanceToUser: Directly relevant to the user's non-malignant hematology interests — NHLBI is the home institute for bone marrow failure, MDS-adjacent marrow biology, and other blood disorders. An established investigator with an NHLBI-funded blood-research program applying AI/ML methods would target this. Requires an existing NHLBI R01 track record, so it is a later-career mechanism.

name: NHLBI Emerging Investigator Award (EIA) (R35)
funder: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI), NIH
awardAmount: Varies — budget based on the PI's current NHLBI funding level, up to the NOFO maximum. See site.
deadlineInfo: Current cycle RFA-HL-26-003; application due date February 19, 2026. Reissued periodically.
eligibility: Early-stage / emerging investigators: one qualifying grant must be an NHLBI-funded NIH Early Stage Investigator award, with four consecutive years of NHLBI support. Single-PI; minimum 50% research effort.
description: The early-career counterpart to the NHLBI OIA — long-term, flexible R35 program support for emerging investigators transitioning from their first NHLBI ESI award to stable program funding.
relevanceToUser: The early-career path to stable NHLBI program funding for a researcher building an AI/ML program in non-malignant hematology / bone marrow failure. Requires having first secured an NHLBI ESI R01-equivalent award, so it is a stepping-stone after initial NHLBI funding rather than a first grant.

name: NIGMS Maximizing Investigators' Research Award (MIRA) (R35)
funder: National Institute of General Medical Sciences (NIGMS), NIH
awardAmount: New/Early-Stage Investigator MIRA budgets typically ~$275,000 direct costs/year; established-investigator budgets vary with prior NIGMS funding. 5-year awards.
deadlineInfo: Ongoing NOFOs with multiple annual due dates. Established/experienced-investigator MIRA: PAR-26-121. Early Stage Investigators apply through a separate ESI MIRA NOFO (recently PAR-23-145). Confirm current due dates on the NIGMS MIRA page.
eligibility: For investigators whose research aligns with the NIGMS mission (basic, non-disease-specific biomedical science). Established track: PD/PIs holding an eligible NIGMS single-PI R01-equivalent (R01, R37, DP1, DP2, SC1). Separate ESI track for early-stage investigators. Single grant consolidating NIGMS support; increased stability and flexibility.
description: An Outstanding-Investigator-style R35 that provides a single, flexible, stable grant for an investigator's NIGMS-mission research program instead of multiple project grants, with separate tracks for early-stage and established investigators.
relevanceToUser: Relevant only if the AI/ML methods work is framed as basic, broadly applicable biomedical science (e.g., general computational/statistical methodology) rather than cancer-specific — NIGMS does not fund disease-targeted oncology research, which belongs to NCI/NHLBI. A methods-first ML researcher whose tools generalize across biology could pursue MIRA; a strictly oncology-focused program would not fit.

name: EvansMDS Discovery Research Grant (DRG)
awardAmount: Up to $250,000 per year for up to 3 years (up to ~$750,000 total)
deadlineInfo: Annual, two-stage (LOI then invited full app). 2026 cycle: LOI portal opened Dec 1, 2025; LOI deadline Jan 31, 2026; invited full applications due Apr 15, 2026; awards mid-June 2026; funding begins Sept 1, 2026. Next (2027) cycle LOI portal expected to open ~Nov/Dec 2026.
eligibility: Established independent investigators in a tenure-track or tenured position at the Assistant Professor level (or equivalent) or higher, at a recognized 501(c)(3) university or non-profit institution in the United States. PhD, MD, or equivalent. Non-US Assistant-Professor-level investigators may apply subject to a US equivalency determination. One LOI per year; prior EvansMDS Young Investigator awardees' LOIs are auto-accepted for 2 years after their EYIA funding ends.
description: Competitive grants funding collaborative, transformative, high-impact research into the etiology, pathogenesis, and treatment of MDS. Priority for projects directly examining MDS rather than general leukemia/hematopoiesis/aging concepts.
relevanceToUser: The strongest fit in this cluster for an AI/ML computational oncology researcher: the official 2026 RFA explicitly names two suggested topics of interest that are squarely computational/AI — 'Creating new curated data sources and novel computational tools for the application of machine learning to MDS research' and 'Novel imaging and pathology modalities and algorithms for improving diagnosis, understanding pathogenesis, and directing MDS therapy.' Applies once the researcher holds an independent faculty (Assistant Professor+) position; requires framing the project around MDS.

name: EvansMDS Young Investigator Award (EYIA)
careerStages: senior/late-stage trainee transitioning to independent faculty
awardAmount: Up to $150,000 per year for up to 3 years (up to ~$450,000 total; salary + supplies)
duration: Up to 3 years (years 2-3 conditional on progress toward and securing an independent tenure-track position)
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2026 cycle: submission portal opened Dec 1, 2025; deadline Jan 31, 2026; notification late March 2026; funding begins July 1, 2026. 2027 application cycle opens November 2026.
eligibility: Early-career physician or scientist (MD, PhD, or MD/PhD), typically within 9 years of completing their last graduate degree, currently in a non-tenure-track (or equivalent) position at a US 501(c)(3) or equivalent non-profit institution — aimed at late-stage postdoctoral fellows with a strong publication record transitioning to independence. Should not yet hold the title of Assistant Professor (and not Associate/Full Professor). Individuals who have been PI on a government or privately funded research grant are ineligible (NIH training grants and federal/foundation career-development awards excepted). Requires an established MDS-investigator mentor. Application via ProposalCentral.
description: A transition-to-independence award to develop the next generation of MDS research leaders, supporting study of the biology, pathogenesis, and treatment of MDS as senior trainees move to independent investigator positions.
relevanceToUser: Best-fit mechanism for a computational/ML researcher at the postdoc-to-faculty transition stage who wants to commit to MDS. Computational/AI projects on MDS biology, genomics, or prognosis qualify if framed around MDS and mentored by an established MDS investigator. Successful EYIA awardees get a non-competitive fast track into the larger Discovery Research Grant program.

name: MDS Foundation Young Investigator Grant
funder: The Myelodysplastic Syndromes Foundation, Inc. (MDS Foundation)
awardAmount: $150,000 total for 2 years, paid in two equal $75,000 installments to the awardee's institution
deadlineInfo: Annual. In the most recent (2025) cycle: applications opened Jan 8, deadline Feb 28 (11:59pm ET), notification Apr 9, grant term Jul 1 2025 - Jun 30 2027. Cycle is currently closed between rounds — sign up for alerts on the site; next-cycle exact dates to be confirmed on the funder page.
eligibility: Investigator (US or international) early in career: PD/PI who completed their terminal research degree or postgraduate clinical training (whichever is later) within the past 10 years AND has not previously competed successfully as PD/PI for a substantial independent research award (e.g., NIH R01, Leukemia & Lymphoma Society grant, or equivalent). One extra year allowed for time as chief resident or in military service. Project must address MDS or related myeloid neoplasms (basic or translational). Applicant MUST be an MDS Foundation professional member in good standing or the application will not be reviewed. Apply via ProposalCentral.
description: Early-career research award to initiate, continue, or complete a basic or clinical-management project on the causation, epidemiology, molecular biology, cytogenetics, morphology, prognosis, or treatment of MDS and related myeloid neoplasms.
relevanceToUser: Well-suited to an early-career computational/ML researcher (within 10 years of PhD/MD, no prior R01-level award) doing MDS-focused work — the scope explicitly includes molecular biology, cytogenetics, prognosis, and morphology, all amenable to ML/computational methods. Note the requirement to be a dues-paying MDS Foundation professional member before applying.

name: MDS Pilot Research Grants
funder: The Taub Foundation for MDS Research
awardAmount: $100,000 over 1 year (two $50,000 payments); indirect costs NOT covered
deadlineInfo: Annual, two-stage (LOI then invited full app). In the 2025 cycle: portal opened Jan 1, 2025; Letters of Intent due Mar 10, 2025; full applications due Jul 1, 2025; awards Sept 26, 2025; funding Dec 1, 2025 - Nov 30, 2026. Expect a comparable schedule for the next cycle; confirm dates on site.
eligibility: Independent early-career investigators holding a faculty position (instructor level or above) with designated laboratory space at a US non-profit institution (university, college, hospital, or research lab; for-profit entities excluded). Neither US citizenship nor visa documentation required. Projects must address MDS through transformative basic or translational research (clinical studies excluded; work may study MDS-to-AML progression but cannot primarily focus on AML or MPN). Two-stage process designed to require little/no preliminary data.
description: Pilot grants for high-risk, high-reward basic and translational MDS research from independent early-career faculty, intentionally structured to fund bold ideas that lack preliminary data.
relevanceToUser: Relevant for an early-career faculty member applying ML/computational methods to MDS basic or translational biology. The 'little/no preliminary data' design is friendly to novel methodological/computational ideas. Note: clinical studies are excluded and the project must be MDS-centric (not AML/MPN-focused).

name: MDS Research Fund (Early Career & Established Investigator Awards)
awardAmount: Early Career Award: up to $125,000/year for 2 years (~$250K). Established Investigator Award: up to $250,000/year for 2 years (~$500K). Typically 3 grants/year funded (2 Early Career + 1 Established).
deadlineInfo: Annual; calls for proposals are announced on the foundation's website (specific deadline not published in the general description — see site for current cycle). Applications reviewed by a Scientific Advisory Board.
eligibility: Early Career: completed terminal doctoral degree (PhD, MD, or equivalent) or postgraduate clinical training within the past 10 years, with no prior NIH R01 or equivalent funding. Established Investigator: those not qualifying as Early Career (or previous Early Career awardees). Investigators must be at a university, college, hospital, or research institution and remain there for the grant's first year. Current Dresner grantees may not apply or serve as co-investigators on new applications. Research must directly address MDS and related blood disorders; proposals focused on de novo AML or MPN are excluded.
description: Funds innovative basic-science, translational, or clinical research directly addressing MDS and related blood disorders, across two career tiers (Early Career and Established Investigator).
relevanceToUser: Covers both early-career faculty and established investigators, so it fits a computational/ML researcher at either stage doing MDS-directed work (basic, translational, or clinical). Larger Established Investigator tier could support a substantial computational-oncology MDS program. Must be MDS-centric (AML/MPN-only projects excluded).

name: New Investigator Research Grant Program
awardAmount: Up to $150,000 total (up to $75,000/year over 2 years). No PI salary support; no indirect/overhead; equipment >$7,500 restricted; research travel capped at $3,000.
deadlineInfo: Annual cycle. 2026 cycle: LOI portal opened Dec 5, 2025; LOI due Feb 6, 2026 (12pm CT); full-application invitations Mar 13, 2026; full application due Apr 13, 2026 (12pm CT); awards notified by Jun 30, 2026. Submitted via ProposalCentral.
eligibility: Independent investigators at a non-profit academic institution within 7 years of the start of their first independent faculty-level appointment (assistant professor or equivalent); residency/fellowship/postdoc years do NOT count toward the 7 years. Must hold an independent position (dedicated lab space, staff supervision, research decision-making). Cannot hold active grants totaling >=$175K/year direct costs (e.g., NIH R01, VA Merit); smaller grants such as K08/K99/R00, R21, DoD, or foundation grants under $175K/yr are allowed. No US citizenship or residency requirement; international institutions eligible. Prior LRF grant recipients and co-PIs are ineligible; one LOI per applicant.
description: Leukemia-specific early-career faculty grant supporting highly innovative projects across broad, essential areas of leukemia research; MDS proposals are also accepted. Run by the Leukemia Research Foundation (headquartered in Northfield, IL, in the Chicago metro area).
relevanceToUser: This is the assigned target program and a strong fit: it is leukemia/MDS-specific, methodology-agnostic (computational, AI/ML, and data-science approaches to leukemia biology, pathogenesis, or treatment are eligible), and does not require US citizenship. Applicable once the researcher holds an independent faculty appointment (assistant professor); not available to PhD students/postdocs. Distinct from the Lymphoma Research Foundation.

name: ASH Scholar Award (Fellow Scholar and Junior Faculty Scholar levels)
awardAmount: Fellow Scholar: up to $100,000 over 2-3 years. Junior Faculty Scholar: up to $150,000 over 2-3 years (max $75,000/year). Paid in equal quarterly installments (partial salary/research support).
deadlineInfo: 2027 activation cycle: LOI due May 1, 2026; invited full application due Aug 3, 2026; notification Jan 2027; award activation Jul 2027. Annual.
eligibility: PhD, PharmD, MD/DO, or MD-PhD hematology researchers. Fellow level: PhD/PharmD within ~4-6 years of terminal degree, or MD/DO in mentored postdoctoral/fellow positions (>=6 to <11 yrs post-residency). Junior Faculty level: >8 to <=13 years of post-degree research experience, or anyone holding an independent faculty (assistant professor) position must apply at the Junior Faculty level. At award acceptance, <=$150,000/yr of other research funding (excluding salary/fringe and internal funds). Requires ASH membership; two mentors for Fellow level.
description: Long-running (since 1985) ASH award providing partial salary/research support during the transition from training to an independent hematology research career, at both fellow and junior-faculty levels.
relevanceToUser: Open to PhD (non-MD) hematology researchers, so a computational/ML scientist studying blood cancers can qualify at the Fellow level (postdoc) or Junior Faculty level (early-career faculty). Hematologic-malignancy computational research is in scope. Requires ASH membership.

name: ASH Fellow-to-Faculty Scholar Award
awardAmount: Up to $125,000 over 2-3 years, paid in equal quarterly installments.
deadlineInfo: 2027 activation cycle: LOI due May 1, 2026; invited full application due Aug 3, 2026. Annual.
eligibility: Postdoctoral hematology researchers who are citizens of the USA or Canada (research may be conducted at institutions worldwide), preparing for their first independent group-leader position. MD/DO, PhD, or MD-PhD applicants generally >=6 to <11 years post-residency; PhD/PharmD >=6 to <11 years post terminal degree. Must be in mentored postdoctoral positions and be ASH members.
description: ASH transition award helping senior postdocs in hematology bridge to their first independent faculty position.
relevanceToUser: Well-suited to a senior computational/ML postdoc working on blood cancers who is targeting a first faculty position; disease and methodology fit are strong. Note: restricted to US/Canada citizens and requires ASH membership.

name: EvansMDS Discovery Research Grant
awardAmount: Up to $250,000 per year for up to 3 years.
deadlineInfo: Annual. 2026 cycle closed Jan 31, 2026; 2027 cycle opens November 2026 (RFA on the EvansMDS site).
eligibility: Independent investigators addressing critical challenges in myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS). Confirm detailed eligibility/citizenship in the current RFA.
description: Larger MDS-focused project grant for studies addressing key challenges in myelodysplastic syndromes.
relevanceToUser: A larger MDS mechanism suited to an independent (faculty) investigator; relevant as the researcher's lab matures, and computational/AI methods applied to MDS genomics/pathogenesis are in scope. Less applicable to trainees.

name: NLM Research Grants: Advancing Bioinformatics, Translational Bioinformatics and Computational Biology Research (R01 Clinical Trial Optional) — PAR-26-040
funder: NIH National Library of Medicine (NLM), with Office of Data Science Strategy
awardAmount: Up to $250,000 per year in direct costs (approx. $2.5M program budget, ~10 awards expected per cycle)
deadlineInfo: Standard NIH due dates recurring annually — Feb 5, June 5, Oct 5 (next: June 5, 2026); NOFO active through expiration March 6, 2029. Merit review ~summer, earliest start ~Dec after submission.
eligibility: U.S. higher-education institutions, nonprofits, for-profits/small businesses, and government/tribal entities. Foreign organizations may apply but foreign subawards/subcontracts are not allowed. PI need not be a U.S. citizen. No new/early-stage-investigator restriction.
description: NLM R01 for novel, generalizable computational/bioinformatics methods and tools that transform biomedical data into knowledge — explicitly including AI/ML for predictive and analytical bioinformatics, single-cell and spatial omics, systems biology, biomarker and drug-target discovery, and personalized medicine. This is one of two NLM R01s that succeeded the broader (now-expired) PAR-25-238 Biomedical Informatics & Data Science R01.
relevanceToUser: Best-fit NLM R01 for the user's profile: it directly funds AI/ML methods applied to computational biology and omics, which maps cleanly onto computational-oncology and blood-cancer (leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma/MDS) modeling using genomic, transcriptomic, and single-cell data. Method-generalizability and open-source deliverables are exactly what a statistics/ML lab produces.

name: NLM Research Grants in Clinical Informatics (R01 Clinical Trial Optional) — PAR-26-042
awardAmount: Up to $250,000 per year in direct costs (~$2.5M program budget, ~10 awards expected)
deadlineInfo: Standard NIH due dates recurring annually — Feb 5, June 5, Oct 5 (next: June 5, 2026); active through expiration March 6, 2029.
eligibility: U.S. higher-education institutions, nonprofits, for-profits/small businesses, government and tribal entities; foreign entities allowed but no foreign subawards. PI need not be a U.S. citizen.
description: NLM R01 for domain-independent, scalable, reusable clinical-informatics methods that turn complex health data (EHRs, clinical notes, imaging, patient-generated data) into actionable knowledge and decision support for precision/personalized care. The clinical-data counterpart to PAR-26-040.
relevanceToUser: Relevant if the oncology/hematologic-malignancy work touches clinical data — e.g., ML on EHR, clinical notes, or pathology/imaging for leukemia/MDS prognosis, treatment response, or decision support. Complements the bioinformatics R01 when the modeling substrate is clinical rather than molecular.

name: NIH Pathway to Independence Award (Parent K99/R00) — NLM participating (biomedical informatics & data science) — PA-24-194 / PA-24-193
funder: NIH (Parent announcement) — National Library of Medicine participates for informatics/data science
awardAmount: K99 phase: salary + research allowance (IC/institution-dependent, see site). R00 phase: up to ~$249,000/year total costs (salary up to ~$75K, or up to ~$100K for practicing physician-scientists, plus research costs; IC-dependent).
duration: Up to 5 years total: K99 up to 2 years mentored postdoc + R00 up to 3 years as independent faculty
deadlineInfo: New applications: Feb 12, June 12, Oct 12 annually (resubmissions one month later). PA-24-194 = clinical trial not allowed; PA-24-193 = clinical trial required.
eligibility: Postdoctoral researchers with no more than 4 years of postdoc research experience at initial submission (a temporary eligibility extension applies per NOT-OD-25-121). U.S. citizenship NOT required for the K99 (mentored) phase — open to non-citizen/visa holders. NLM gives preference to applicants trained at its university-based informatics/data-science programs.
description: Flagship postdoc-to-faculty transition award. Provides mentored (K99) then independent (R00) support to launch a research program; NLM funds it for careers focused on biomedical informatics, bioinformatics, and data science.
relevanceToUser: Ideal mechanism for a senior postdoc in an ML/statistics lab aiming for a faculty position doing AI applied to computational oncology/blood cancers. NLM's informatics/data-science track fits AI-methods work, and the K99 phase's openness to non-U.S. citizens is a key advantage over most NIH training awards.

name: NLM Institutional Grants for Research Training in Biomedical Informatics, Data Science, and AI (T15) — RFA-LM-26-004
awardAmount: Institutional grant: roughly $500,000–$775,000 per year per program (~25 awards, ~$12M total). Individual trainees receive NRSA stipends, tuition, and training-related expenses.
duration: 5 years (institutional program); predoctoral and postdoctoral trainee appointments within the program
deadlineInfo: RFA-LM-26-004 issued Feb 2026; estimated application due date ~Sept 25, 2026 (one-time RFA cycle; earliest project start ~July 2027). Verify exact date on the RFA.
eligibility: Awarded to institutions, not individuals — a researcher benefits by being a trainee (predoc/postdoc) in a funded program, or a faculty member can serve as program director/faculty. Appointed trainees must be U.S. citizens or permanent residents.
description: NLM's institutional training program (T15) supporting predoctoral and postdoctoral trainees in biomedical informatics, data science, and AI/ML across ~20 U.S. universities (e.g., Harvard/MIT, Stanford, Johns Hopkins, Utah). The 2026 reissue explicitly adds AI/ML to the scope.
relevanceToUser: For a PhD student or postdoc, the practical path is to be appointed as a trainee on an existing NLM T15 slot (your institution-area programs include Harvard/your institution and Boston-area institutions) — funding plus a formal informatics/data-science/AI curriculum. Faculty could join or lead a T15 program. Note the U.S. citizen/permanent-resident requirement for trainee appointments.

name: NLM Career Development Award in Biomedical Informatics and Data Science (K01) / NIH Parent K01
awardAmount: Historically: up to $100,000/year salary plus fringe + $50,000/year research development costs (verify current amounts on the active announcement).
duration: Typically 3 years (up to 5 for some Parent K01 versions)
deadlineInfo: See site — the dedicated NLM K01 PAR (e.g., PAR-16-204) has lapsed; NLM applicants can use the NIH Parent K01 (standard due dates Feb 12, June 12, Oct 12). Confirm current NLM participation on the NLM career/training funding page.
eligibility: Junior investigators with a health-professional or research doctorate, typically within the first ~2 years of an initial assistant-professor-level appointment. NLM encourages applicants trained at its university-based informatics programs. Generally requires U.S. citizenship/permanent residency (K-award standard).
description: Mentored career-development award providing protected time and salary/research support for early-stage informatics/data-science investigators to reach independence and R01 competitiveness. The NLM-branded K01 announcement appears to have lapsed; the NIH Parent K01 remains available with NLM among participating institutes.
relevanceToUser: A career-development option for a newly-appointed faculty member (or physician-scientist) building an AI/data-science program in computational oncology who needs mentored protected time before an R01. Lower confidence: verify that a current NLM-specific or Parent K01 route with NLM participation is open before relying on it.

name: Amazon Research Awards (ARA)
awardAmount: Varies by call. Unrestricted cash gift typically up to ~$80,000 (up to $100,000 for some tracks e.g. Amazon 2030) PLUS AWS promotional credits up to ~$50,000–$70,000. Structured as an unrestricted gift to the PI's institution (no IP claimed, no overhead).
duration: One-time unrestricted gift; research expected to show real-world impact within ~3–5 years
deadlineInfo: Two calls per year — Spring (opens ~late March, closes ~mid-May; Spring 2026 closed May 13, 2026, decisions Aug 2026) and Fall (closes ~mid-November). Rotating topic tracks (AI for science, Agentic AI, Think Big, Build on Trainium, security, robotics, etc.).
eligibility: PI must be a full-time faculty member at an accredited academic institution (or a permanent researcher at a 501(c)(3)-equivalent nonprofit) and at the age of majority. Co-PIs must also be faculty/permanent researchers. Not open to PhD students or postdocs as PI; cannot be a recent Amazon employee/contractor. One proposal per PI per call.
description: Amazon's academic research program giving unrestricted funds plus AWS compute credits, access to Amazon public datasets, and an Amazon technical contact. Health/AI-for-science tracks have funded biomedical work (e.g., deep-learning models with GPU/AWS compute, drug-discovery and clinical-prediction projects).
relevanceToUser: Strong fit for compute-heavy AI/ML oncology work: the AWS credits directly subsidize GPU training of deep-learning models on genomic/single-cell/clinical data, and 'AI for science'/'Think Big'/health-sciences tracks welcome computational-biology methods. Requires a full-time faculty PI — a lab PI applies, not a student/postdoc directly.

name: Wellcome Leap — Health Breakthrough Programs (performer funding)
funder: Wellcome Leap Inc. (nonprofit, funded by Wellcome Trust)
awardAmount: Varies by program and milestone (see site). Programs are 'funded at scale' with large pools (individual programs ~$45M–$60M total); individual performer contract awards are milestone-based and vary — no fixed per-award figure published.
deadlineInfo: Program-specific and rolling — opportunities open/close as each program launches its calls. Check the current open-programs list (e.g., VISIBLE full proposals were due Apr 23, 2026). No single recurring deadline.
eligibility: Open globally, including U.S. universities/research institutions and companies; performers can be academic, nonprofit, or for-profit. Applicants need not belong to the Wellcome Leap Health Breakthrough Network. Milestone/deliverable-driven contracts rather than traditional grants.
description: Wellcome Leap runs bold, time-limited 'moonshot' health programs seeking breakthroughs on aggressive timelines. AI/ML- and data-science-heavy programs include Delta Tissue (predicting disease state transitions from multimodal data / precision medicine) and Q4Bio (quantum computing for biology); R3 touches mRNA/cancer. Programs open and close over time.
relevanceToUser: A stretch but legitimately AI/ML-relevant option: Delta Tissue and similar data-prediction programs reward computational methods for modeling disease progression, which is adjacent to computational-oncology and hematologic-malignancy trajectory modeling. There is no current dedicated blood-cancer program, and awards are milestone contracts — monitor the open-programs page for a call that matches the user's methods. Low confidence on amounts/deadlines because these are program-specific.
